abstract:
Recent vision-language-action models (VLAs) build upon pretrained
vision-language models and leverage diverse robot datasets to demonstrate
strong task execution, language following ability, and semantic generalization.
Despite these successes, VLAs struggle with novel robot setups and require
fine-tuning to achieve good performance, yet how to most effectively fine-tune
them is unclear given many possible strategies. In this work, we study key VLA
adaptation design choices such as different action decoding schemes, action
representations, and learning objectives for fine-tuning, using OpenVLA as our
representative base model. Our empirical analysis informs an Optimized
Fine-Tuning (OFT) recipe that integrates parallel decoding, action chunking, a
continuous action representation, and a simple L1 regression-based learning
objective to altogether improve inference efficiency, policy performance, and
flexibility in the model's input-output specifications. We propose OpenVLA-OFT,
an instantiation of this recipe, which sets a new state of the art on the
LIBERO simulation benchmark, significantly boosting OpenVLA's average success
rate across four task suites from 76.5% to 97.1% while increasing action
generation throughput by 26$\times$. In real-world evaluations, our fine-tuning
recipe enables OpenVLA to successfully execute dexterous, high-frequency
control tasks on a bimanual ALOHA robot and outperform other VLAs ($\pi_0$ and
RDT-1B) fine-tuned using their default recipes, as well as strong imitation
learning policies trained from scratch (Diffusion Policy and ACT) by up to 15%
(absolute) in average success rate. We release code for OFT and pretrained
model checkpoints at this https URL

keyInsights: Combining parallel decoding with action chunking drastically improves both VLA inference efficiency and policy performance, increasing OpenVLA's success rate by 14% on LIBERO.
Using continuous action representations with a simple L1 regression objective yields comparable task performance to diffusion-based methods while offering significantly faster training and inference.
A well-designed fine-tuning strategy enables VLAs pretrained on single-arm data to successfully adapt and even outperform models with bimanual pretraining on complex bimanual manipulation tasks.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often have slow inference speeds (e.g., 3-5 Hz), making them unsuitable for high-frequency robotic control (25-50+ Hz) required for dexterous manipulation.
Current VLA fine-tuning methods show unsatisfactory performance on complex tasks, particularly in bimanual manipulation scenarios.
There is a lack of clear, empirically-validated guidance on how to effectively fine-tune VLAs for optimal speed and success on novel robot setups and tasks.
results: OpenVLA-OFT achieved a new state of the art on the LIBERO simulation benchmark with an average success rate of 97.1% (up from 76.5%) and a 26x speedup in action generation throughput (109.7 Hz).

On a real ALOHA bimanual robot, OpenVLA-OFT+ successfully executed diverse dexterous tasks, consistently outperforming other fine-tuned VLAs and achieving up to 15% higher average success rates, despite being pretrained only on single-arm data.
The inclusion of Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) was essential for robust language grounding on the ALOHA robot, preventing language-dependent task success from plummeting to chance levels.
solution: A systematic empirical analysis was conducted on OpenVLA, evaluating key design choices for VLA fine-tuning: action generation strategy (autoregressive vs. parallel decoding with action chunking), action representation (discrete vs. continuous), and learning objective (L1 regression vs. diffusion).

An "Optimized Fine-Tuning (OFT) recipe" was developed, integrating parallel decoding, action chunking, continuous action representation, and a simple L1 regression objective for efficient and high-performance VLA adaptation.

Architectural modifications were introduced to support multiple camera views and low-dimensional robot state inputs, with Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) added to the OFT+ variant for enhanced language grounding.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Stanford University developed an Optimized Fine-Tuning (OFT) recipe for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, substantially increasing their inference speed and task success on new robotic setups, including complex bimanual manipulation, by integrating parallel decoding, action chunking, and L1 regression with continuous actions.

title: Fine-Tuning Vision-Language-Action Models: Optimizing Speed and Success

abstract: Modern AI systems, especially those interacting with the physical world, increasingly require real-time performance. However, the high latency of state-of-the-art generalist models, including recent vision-language action models (VLAs), poses a significant challenge. While action chunking has enabled temporal consistency in high-frequency control tasks, it does not fully address the latency problem, leading to pauses or out-of-distribution jerky movements at chunk boundaries. This paper presents a novel inference-time algorithm that enables smooth asynchronous execution of action chunking policies. Our method, real-time chunking (RTC), is applicable to any diffusion- or flow-based VLA out of the box with no re-training. It generates the next action chunk while executing the current one, "freezing" actions guaranteed to execute and "inpainting" the rest. To test RTC, we introduce a new benchmark of 12 highly dynamic tasks in the Kinetix simulator, as well as evaluate 6 challenging real-world bimanual manipulation tasks. Results demonstrate that RTC is fast, performant, and uniquely robust to inference delay, significantly improving task throughput and enabling high success rates in precise tasks $\unicode{x2013}$ such as lighting a match $\unicode{x2013}$ even in the presence of significant latency. See this https URL for videos.

keyInsights: The challenge of high-latency policy execution in real-time robotics can be effectively addressed by reframing it as an inpainting problem, leveraging the inherent capabilities of diffusion and flow models.
Soft masking, with its gradual weighting scheme, is crucial for achieving superior cross-chunk continuity and preventing discontinuities or 'strategy switching' compared to binary hard masking.

An inference-time-only algorithm can robustly mitigate severe latency in complex Vision-Language-Action models, making them practical for demanding real-time physical tasks without requiring costly model retraining.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models incur high inference latency, hindering their effective deployment in real-time robotic control.
Existing action chunking methods, while improving temporal consistency, lead to issues like pauses, mode-jumping, and jerky movements at chunk boundaries due to latency.
Naive asynchronous strategies or simple smoothing techniques (e.g., temporal ensembling) can exacerbate latency issues or produce invalid actions, especially with multi-modal action distributions.
results: RTC demonstrated remarkable robustness to inference delays in simulation, consistently outperforming all baselines and maintaining high task success rates even when 50% of the prediction horizon was delayed.

On real-world bimanual manipulation tasks, RTC achieved the best average task throughput and complete robustness to injected latencies up to +200ms, while synchronous inference degraded linearly and other baselines failed entirely.

The method enabled faster task completion, higher success rates, and smoother execution, significantly reducing retries in precision-sensitive real-world manipulation tasks such as 'light candle' and 'bed making'.
solution: RTC formulates asynchronous policy execution as an inpainting task, leveraging generative models (diffusion/flow) to maintain continuity between action chunks.

It employs gradient-based guidance (ΠGDM with a crucial clipping mechanism) integrated into the learned velocity field, aligning newly generated chunks with actions already committed from the previous chunk.

A novel soft masking technique, using exponentially decaying weights across chunk boundaries, provides a stronger and smoother guidance signal to ensure continuous strategy execution without requiring retraining of the base VLA policy.
paper_summary.summary: Real-Time Chunking (RTC) is an inference-time algorithm developed by Physical Intelligence and UC Berkeley that enables smooth, asynchronous execution of action chunking policies for high-latency diffusion- and flow-based Vision-Language-Action models. By treating policy execution as an inpainting problem with soft masking, RTC achieves robust performance and high task throughput on both simulated and real-world bimanual manipulation tasks, even with significant inference delays.

title: Real-Time Execution of Action Chunking Flow Policies

abstract: Recent large-scale Vision Language Action (VLA) models have shown superior performance in robotic manipulation tasks guided by natural language. However, current VLA models suffer from two drawbacks: (i) generation of massive tokens leading to high inference latency and increased training cost, and (ii) insufficient utilization of generated actions resulting in potential performance loss. To address these issues, we develop a training framework to finetune VLA models for generating significantly fewer action tokens with high parallelism, effectively reducing inference latency and training cost. Furthermore, we introduce an inference optimization technique with a novel voting-based ensemble strategy to combine current and previous action predictions, improving the utilization of generated actions and overall performance. Our results demonstrate that we achieve superior performance compared with state-of-the-art VLA models, achieving significantly higher success rates and 39$\times$ faster inference than OpenVLA with 46 Hz throughput on edge platforms, demonstrating practical deployability. The code is available at this https URL.
title: VOTE: Vision-Language-Action Optimization with Trajectory Ensemble Voting

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated strong multi-modal reasoning capabilities, enabling direct action generation from visual perception and language instructions in an end-to-end manner. However, their substantial computational cost poses a challenge for real-time robotic control, where rapid decision-making is essential. This paper introduces VLA-Cache, a training-free inference acceleration method that reduces computational overhead by adaptively caching and reusing static visual tokens across frames. Exploiting the temporal continuity in robotic manipulation, VLA-Cache identifies minimally changed tokens between adjacent frames and reuses their cached key-value representations, thereby circumventing redundant computations. Additionally, to maintain action precision, VLA-Cache selectively re-computes task-relevant tokens that are environmentally sensitive, ensuring the fidelity of critical visual information. To further optimize efficiency, we introduce a layer adaptive token reusing strategy that dynamically adjusts the reuse ratio based on attention concentration across decoder layers, prioritizing critical tokens for recomputation. Extensive experiments on two simulation platforms (LIBERO and SIMPLER) and a real-world robotic system demonstrate that VLA-Cache achieves up to 1.7x speedup in CUDA latency and a 15% increase in control frequency, with negligible loss on task success rate. The code and videos can be found at our project page: this https URL.

keyInsights: Temporal continuity and spatial redundancy in sequential visual observations within robotic tasks offer significant opportunities for cross-frame computational reuse.
Distinguishing between visually static but task-relevant tokens and genuinely irrelevant static tokens is crucial; naive reuse of all static tokens degrades performance.
Cross-attention scores from the VLA model's language decoder can effectively and efficiently serve as a proxy for identifying task-relevant visual regions.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art VLA models have high computational costs, making real-time deployment in time-critical robotic control applications challenging.
Existing VLA models redundantly recompute all visual tokens at every timestep, even for static scene elements, leading to inefficiencies.
Prior acceleration methods often require architectural modifications, extensive retraining, or degrade spatial fidelity crucial for precise robotic manipulation.
results: Achieved up to 1.63x speedup in CUDA latency and a 27.31% reduction in FLOPs for OpenVLA, increasing control frequency from 4.23 Hz to 4.59 Hz with only a 0.3% average drop in success rate.
Demonstrated additive gains when applied to high-frequency models, boosting OpenVLA-OFT's control frequency from 65.10 Hz to 78.98 Hz.

Improved the average success rate by 2.4% (from 82.1% to 84.6%) on a real Kinova Jaco2 robot across four manipulation tasks and maintained baseline performance under dynamic backgrounds while reducing FLOPs by 42%.
solution: VLA-Cache implements a training-free inference acceleration method that reuses Key-Value (KV) representations of visually static tokens across consecutive frames.
It identifies static tokens based on patch-level cosine similarity and filters out 'task-relevant' static tokens by analyzing cross-attention scores from the language decoder.
A layer-adaptive token reusing strategy, guided by attention entropy, dynamically adjusts the reuse proportion across different decoder layers to further optimize efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: VLA-Cache introduces an adaptive token caching framework that reduces the computational overhead of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by intelligently reusing static and non-task-relevant visual tokens across consecutive frames. The method achieves up to 1.63x speedup in CUDA latency and a 27.31% FLOP reduction in simulations with minimal performance drop, and improves real-world robot success rates by 2.4%.

title: VLA-Cache: Efficient Vision-Language-Action Manipulation via Adaptive Token Caching

abstract: Recent advances in robot manipulation have leveraged pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs) and explored integrating 3D spatial signals into these models for effective action prediction, giving rise to the promising vision-language-action (VLA) paradigm. However, most existing approaches overlook the importance of active perception: they typically rely on static, wrist-mounted cameras that provide an end-effector-centric viewpoint. As a result, these models are unable to adaptively select optimal viewpoints or resolutions during task execution, which significantly limits their performance in long-horizon tasks and fine-grained manipulation scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose ActiveVLA, a novel vision-language-action framework that empowers robots with active perception capabilities for high-precision, fine-grained manipulation. ActiveVLA adopts a coarse-to-fine paradigm, dividing the process into two stages: (1) Critical region localization. ActiveVLA projects 3D inputs onto multi-view 2D projections, identifies critical 3D regions, and supports dynamic spatial awareness. (2) Active perception optimization. Drawing on the localized critical regions, ActiveVLA uses an active view selection strategy to choose optimal viewpoints. These viewpoints aim to maximize amodal relevance and diversity while minimizing occlusions. Additionally, ActiveVLA applies a 3D zoom-in to improve resolution in key areas. Together, these steps enable finer-grained active perception for precise manipulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ActiveVLA achieves precise 3D manipulation and outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on three simulation benchmarks. Moreover, ActiveVLA transfers seamlessly to real-world scenarios, enabling robots to learn high-precision tasks in complex environments.

keyInsights: Integrating active perception, specifically dynamic viewpoint selection and 3D zoom-in, significantly enhances a robot's ability to overcome occlusions and achieve fine-grained manipulation.
A hierarchical coarse-to-fine perception strategy (identifying a crucial region, then actively refining observation of it) is effective for improving precision and robustness in VLA models.

Active perception, while introducing a moderate increase in inference time, yields substantial improvements in task success rates and generalization across diverse manipulation benchmarks and real-world scenarios.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models primarily rely on passive perception from fixed camera viewpoints, limiting adaptability to dynamic environments.
Passive perception leads to challenges in handling occlusions, acquiring fine-grained details for precise manipulation, and maintaining robustness in cluttered or complex scenarios.
Robots struggle with tasks requiring adaptive observation strategies, as they cannot actively adjust their sensory input to the task's demands.
results: Achieved new state-of-the-art on RLBench with an average success rate of 91.8% and on COLOSSEUM with 65.9%, outperforming previous methods in visual generalization and robustness.
Demonstrated strong performance on GemBench, with an overall average success rate of 51.3%, improving upon the previous state-of-the-art by 1.3 percentage points for long-horizon tasks.

Validated on a real-world KINOVA GEN2 robot, ActiveVLA successfully performs complex, occlusion-heavy tasks (e.g., retrieving a towel, stacking blocks) with high accuracy and stability, significantly surpassing baselines like TriVLA and RVT-2.
solution: ActiveVLA introduces a coarse-to-fine active perception design, starting with 3D Crucial Area Perception to identify task-relevant regions using multi-view rendering and a VLM backbone.

It implements 3D Active Perception through Active Viewpoint Selection, which generates and evaluates candidate camera positions based on visibility, distance, and diversity, and Active 3D Zoom-in, which re-renders crucial areas with a narrowed field of view.
A 3D Action Prediction module utilizes refined VLM outputs from actively perceived views to predict translational targets, rotation, gripper state, and collision flags for precise robot control.
paper_summary.summary: Fudan University researchers developed ActiveVLA, a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework that injects active perception into 3D robotic manipulation by enabling robots to actively select optimal camera viewpoints and dynamically zoom in on crucial regions. This approach achieves new state-of-the-art success rates of 91.8% on RLBench and 65.9% on COLOSSEUM, and demonstrates robust performance in real-world occlusion-heavy tasks.

title: ActiveVLA: Injecting Active Perception into Vision-Language-Action Models for Precise 3D Robotic Manipulation

abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have shown remarkable capabilities in robotic manipulation, but their performance is sensitive to the $\textbf{action chunk length}$ used during training, termed $\textbf{horizon}$. Our empirical study reveals an inherent trade-off: longer horizons provide stronger global foresight but degrade fine-grained accuracy, while shorter ones sharpen local control yet struggle on long-term tasks, implying fixed choice of single horizons being suboptimal. To mitigate the trade-off, we propose a $\textbf{mixture of horizons (MoH)}$ strategy. MoH rearranges the action chunk into several segments with different horizons, processes them in parallel with a shared action transformer, and fuses outputs with a light linear gate. It has three appealing benefits. 1) MoH exploits long-term foresight and short-term precision jointly within a single model, improving both performance and generalizability to complex tasks. 2) MoH is plug-and-play for full-attention action modules with minimal training or inference overhead. 3) MoH enables dynamic inference with adaptive horizons, which selects stable actions through cross-horizon consensus, achieving 2.5$\times$ higher throughput than baselines while preserving superior performance. Extensive experiments over flow-based policies $\pi_0$, $\pi_{0.5}$, and one-step regression policy $\pi_{\text{reg}}$ demonstrate that MoH yields consistent and significant gains on both simulations and real-world tasks. Notably, under mixed-task setting, $\pi_{0.5}$ with MoH reaches a new state-of-the-art with 99$\%$ average success rate on LIBERO after only $30k$ training iterations. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: Integrating multiple horizons effectively reconciles the trade-off between long-term foresight and short-term precision, allowing a single model to excel at both types of tasks.
A learnable gated fusion mechanism, regularized by a balance loss, is crucial for optimally leveraging predictions from different horizons and preventing bias towards longer horizons.

Dynamic inference, guided by the consensus among multiple horizons, enables adaptive control frequency, allowing faster execution during stable phases and more frequent replanning during critical moments.
originalProblem: VLA models using action chunking suffer from a fundamental trade-off: a fixed action chunk horizon is suboptimal, as long horizons excel in long-term tasks while short horizons are better for precise local control.
Existing VLA models typically employ a heuristically chosen, fixed horizon, leading to limited generalization across diverse robotic tasks with varying temporal complexities.
The sensitivity of VLA model performance to the choice of action chunk length means manual tuning is often required for different task types.
results: MoH achieved a new state-of-the-art average success rate of 99% on the LIBERO benchmark with π₀.₅, outperforming existing VLA models, including notable gains on long-horizon tasks (98.4% vs. 95.4% for baseline).
Ablation studies confirmed that the balance loss (L_bal) and a moderately dense set of horizons were critical for MoH's performance, preventing bias and enhancing temporal capture.
Real-robot experiments demonstrated consistent improvements in success rates and more decisive actions for tasks like pouring milk, confirming MoH's practical utility and reduced dithering.
solution: The Mixture of Horizons (MoH) strategy processes and fuses predictions from multiple action chunk lengths concurrently using a shared action transformer with horizon-specific attention masks.
A lightweight linear gating head learns to combine individual horizon-specific predictions into a single fused action prediction, with a balance loss (L_bal) preventing collapse to a single horizon.
MoH incorporates a dynamic inference scheme via horizon consensus, where the model adaptively executes an action prefix based on cross-horizon consistency and uncertainty, rather than a fixed length.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from RUC, UNC, and CUHK introduced Mixture of Horizons (MoH), a strategy that combines predictions from multiple action chunk lengths in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. This approach addresses the trade-off between short-term precision and long-term foresight, achieving a 99% success rate on the LIBERO benchmark and improving real-world robotic manipulation.

title: Mixture of Horizons in Action Chunking

abstract: Recent advances in Visual-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown promising potential for robotic manipulation tasks. However, real-world robotic tasks often involve long-horizon, multi-step problem-solving and require generalization for continual skill acquisition, extending beyond single actions or skills. These challenges present significant barriers for existing VLA models, which use monolithic action decoders trained on aggregated data, resulting in poor scalability. To address these challenges, we propose AtomicVLA, a unified planning-and-execution framework that jointly generates task-level plans, atomic skill abstractions, and fine-grained actions. AtomicVLA constructs a scalable atomic skill library through a Skill-Guided Mixture-of-Experts (SG-MoE), where each expert specializes in mastering generic yet precise atomic skills. Furthermore, we introduce a flexible routing encoder that automatically assigns dedicated atomic experts to new skills, enabling continual learning. We validate our approach through extensive experiments. In simulation, AtomicVLA outperforms $\pi_{0}$ by 2.4\% on LIBERO, 10\% on LIBERO-LONG, and outperforms $\pi_{0}$ and $\pi_{0.5}$ by 0.22 and 0.25 in average task length on CALVIN. Additionally, our AtomicVLA consistently surpasses baselines by 18.3\% and 21\% in real-world long-horizon tasks and continual learning. These results highlight the effectiveness of atomic skill abstraction and dynamic expert composition for long-horizon and lifelong robotic tasks. The project page is \href{this https URL}{here}.

keyInsights: Explicitly decomposing complex tasks into semantically meaningful 'atomic skill abstractions' and dynamically activating specialized experts enhances task reliability and reduces interference among diverse skills.
A unified robot policy that can autonomously decide between high-level planning and fine-grained action generation avoids coordination issues common in decoupled systems.

A physics-informed trajectory-based atomic decomposition method, combined with VLM refinement, produces precise and interpretable annotations for robot actions, leading to higher quality training data.
originalProblem: Existing Visual-Language-Action (VLA) models exhibit limitations in structured task decomposition and planning for long-horizon, multi-step robotic tasks.
Current VLA models rely on monolithic action decoders, leading to scalability issues, computational expense, and catastrophic forgetting when incrementally acquiring new skills.
Decoupled planning and execution systems often result in suboptimal task coordination due to a lack of mutual awareness between high-level planners and low-level controllers.
results: Achieved an average success rate of 96.6% across four LIBERO task suites (2.4% improvement over baselines) and 95.2% on LIBERO-LONG (10% improvement over the "pi_0" baseline) in simulation.

Outperformed baseline models by 20% in average success rate on real-world long-horizon tasks, and maintained stable performance on previously learned tasks (only 1.3% average decrease) during continual learning.

Ablation studies showed the SG-MoE architecture significantly outperformed token-level MoE (by 6.6%) and denoising timestep-conditioned MoDE (by 5.7%) in terms of success rate, validating the effectiveness of skill-guided expert routing.
solution: An end-to-end framework unifies task planning and action execution by adaptively switching between generating high-level language instructions ('think' mode) and low-level latent actions ('act' mode).
A Skill-Guided Mixture of Experts (SG-MoE) architecture extends VLA foundation models, using atomic skill abstractions to dynamically route inputs to specialized experts for precise action execution.

Modular skill experts enable scalable continual learning by adding new expert modules and extending the routing network for new skills without retraining existing components, thus mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
paper_summary.summary: AtomicVLA is an end-to-end framework for robotic manipulation that integrates task planning and action execution through atomic skill abstraction and a Skill-Guided Mixture of Experts (SG-MoE) architecture. This approach demonstrated improved success rates on long-horizon tasks, achieved scalable continual learning, and mitigated catastrophic forgetting in both simulated and real-world robotic environments.

title: AtomicVLA: Unlocking the Potential of Atomic Skill Learning in Robots

abstract: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models rely on fixed computational depth, expending the same amount of compute on simple adjustments and complex multi-step manipulation. While Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting enables variable computation, it scales memory linearly and is ill-suited for continuous action spaces. We introduce Recurrent-Depth VLA (RD-VLA), an architecture that achieves computational adaptivity via latent iterative refinement rather than explicit token generation. RD-VLA employs a recurrent, weight-tied action head that supports arbitrary inference depth with a constant memory footprint. The model is trained using truncated backpropagation through time (TBPTT) to efficiently supervise the refinement process. At inference, RD-VLA dynamically allocates compute using an adaptive stopping criterion based on latent convergence. Experiments on challenging manipulation tasks show that recurrent depth is critical: tasks that fail entirely (0 percent success) with single-iteration inference exceed 90 percent success with four iterations, while simpler tasks saturate rapidly. RD-VLA provides a scalable path to test-time compute in robotics, replacing token-based reasoning with latent reasoning to achieve constant memory usage and up to 80x inference speedup over prior reasoning-based VLA models. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: Robotic reasoning can be efficiently performed in a continuous latent space through iterative refinement, rather than relying on explicit token generation in output space.
A weight-tied recurrent transformer block enables variable computational depth at test time with a constant memory footprint, by repeatedly applying the same neural circuitry.
Training with randomized recurrence and truncated backpropagation through time encourages the model to learn a robust iterative refinement process capable of converging from noisy initial states.
originalProblem: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models traditionally operate with a fixed computational depth, applying the same compute to both simple and complex robotic tasks.
Existing reasoning-centric VLAs rely on explicit token-based reasoning, which is inefficient for continuous action spaces and leads to linear memory scaling with reasoning length.
Deliberation in current reasoning VLAs occurs in discrete output space, causing information bottlenecks and quantization noise during cyclical re-projection from continuous latent representations.
results: RD-VLA achieves state-of-the-art performance on the LIBERO benchmark with 93.0% average success, outperforming larger token-reasoning models.
The adaptive computation strategy reduces average inference compute by 34% while maintaining competitive performance, and provides up to an 80x inference speedup over prior reasoning-based VLAs.

The model demonstrates superior robustness in real-world bimanual robot tasks and achieves the highest average task chain length of 3.39 on the CALVIN ABC →D benchmark, indicating enhanced sequential planning.
solution: Introduces Recurrent-Depth VLA (RD-VLA), which utilizes a weight-tied recurrent transformer core for implicit, iterative reasoning within a continuous latent space.
The model employs a latent "scratchpad" that is iteratively refined by the recurrent core, grounded by VLM features, ensuring representational stability.
An adaptive stopping criterion, based on the Kullback-Leibler divergence of action distributions, dynamically scales test-time computation by determining the required number of recurrent iterations.
paper_summary.summary: Recurrent-Depth VLA (RD-VLA) introduces a method for Vision-Language-Action models to implicitly scale test-time computation through iterative latent-space reasoning, employing a weight-tied recurrent core. This approach achieves state-of-the-art performance with 93.0% average success on the LIBERO benchmark, up to an 80x inference speedup, and demonstrates robust real-world application, all while maintaining a constant memory footprint.

title: Recurrent-Depth VLA: Implicit Test-Time Compute Scaling of Vision-Language-Action Models via Latent Iterative Reasoning

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have become a cornerstone in robotic policy learning, leveraging large-scale multimodal data for robust and scalable control. However, existing VLA frameworks primarily address short-horizon tasks, and their effectiveness on long-horizon, multi-step robotic manipulation remains limited due to challenges in skill chaining and subtask dependencies. In this work, we introduce Long-VLA, the first end-to-end VLA model specifically designed for long-horizon robotic tasks. Our approach features a novel phase-aware input masking strategy that adaptively segments each subtask into moving and interaction phases, enabling the model to focus on phase-relevant sensory cues and enhancing subtask compatibility. This unified strategy preserves the scalability and data efficiency of VLA training, and our architecture-agnostic module can be seamlessly integrated into existing VLA models. We further propose the L-CALVIN benchmark to systematically evaluate long-horizon manipulation. Extensive experiments on both simulated and real-world tasks demonstrate that Long-VLA significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods, establishing a new baseline for long-horizon robotic control.

organization_info.name: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Future Blockchain and Privacy Computing of Electronic Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Explicitly distinguishing and adapting to 'moving' and 'interaction' phases within subtasks can effectively mitigate error propagation and state mismatches in long-horizon robot manipulation.

An input-level adaptation strategy using dynamic masking allows for selective visual attention based on the current task phase, providing better control and robustness during both navigation and precise interaction.
Unifying phase-aware policies within an end-to-end VLA training framework maintains scalability and data efficiency, enabling the model to learn complex, multi-step behaviors jointly.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are primarily designed for short-horizon tasks, limiting their applicability to complex, multi-step robotic operations.
Long-horizon tasks suffer from the skill chaining problem, where errors and state mismatches accumulate across subtasks, leading to overall task failure.
Traditional task decomposition and online adaptive methods for long-horizon tasks often conflict with the end-to-end, large-scale offline training paradigm of VLA models.
results: Long-VLA achieves up to an 81% relative improvement over the base policy for 10-step tasks on the L-CALVIN simulation benchmark and an average task length of 8.24 on the L-CALVIN ABCD->D split, outperforming state-of-the-art VLA models.

In real-world scenarios, Long-VLA maintains nearly 25% success on an 8-step sorting task where the baseline fails completely, and shows a 250% relative improvement for 4-step cleaning tasks under unseen lighting conditions.

Ablation studies confirm the individual contributions of phase decomposition and the input-level adaptation, demonstrating the full Long-VLA model's superior performance due to its unified, data-efficient approach.
solution: Long-VLA introduces an end-to-end VLA model that incorporates a novel phase-aware input masking strategy to handle long-horizon tasks.
Robot trajectories are decomposed into distinct 'moving' and 'interaction' phases, with a phase identifier augmenting the action representation during training and inference.

A dynamic binary masking mechanism is applied to visual input tokens (e.g., third-person views for moving, ego-centric views for interaction), allowing the model to selectively focus on phase-relevant information without altering its core architecture.
paper_summary.summary: Long-VLA enhances Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for long-horizon robot manipulation by introducing a phase-aware input masking strategy and unified training. This approach mitigates the skill chaining problem, achieving up to 81% relative performance improvement in simulations and demonstrating enhanced robustness in real-world tasks like sorting and cleaning, even under visual distractions.

title: Long-VLA: Unleashing Long-Horizon Capability of Vision Language Action Model for Robot Manipulation

abstract: The advancement of large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models has significantly improved robotic manipulation in terms of language-guided task execution and generalization to unseen scenarios. While existing VLAs adapted from pretrained large Vision-Language-Models (VLM) have demonstrated promising generalizability, their task performance is still unsatisfactory as indicated by the low tasks success rates in different environments. In this paper, we present a new advanced VLA architecture derived from VLM. Unlike previous works that directly repurpose VLM for action prediction by simple action quantization, we propose a omponentized VLA architecture that has a specialized action module conditioned on VLM output. We systematically study the design of the action module and demonstrates the strong performance enhancement with diffusion action transformers for action sequence modeling, as well as their favorable scaling behaviors. We also conduct comprehensive experiments and ablation studies to evaluate the efficacy of our models with varied designs. The evaluation on 5 robot embodiments in simulation and real work shows that our model not only significantly surpasses existing VLAs in task performance and but also exhibits remarkable adaptation to new robots and generalization to unseen objects and backgrounds. It exceeds the average success rates of OpenVLA which has similar model size (7B) with ours by over 35% in simulated evaluation and 55% in real robot experiments. It also outperforms the large RT-2-X model (55B) by 18% absolute success rates in simulation. Code and models can be found on our project page (this https URL).

keyInsights: Treating robotic action generation as distinct from language generation, requiring specialized modeling approaches for continuous, multimodal, and temporally correlated actions.

A componentized architecture where a dedicated diffusion transformer action module, conditioned by cognitive features from a VLM, effectively models complex action distributions without discretization.
Adaptive action ensemble during inference can smooth action trajectories and improve robustness by combining multiple predictions based on similarity.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often quantize continuous action spaces into discrete bins, leading to imprecise robotic control.
These models struggle to capture the inherent temporal correlation between successive robot actions and represent multimodal action distributions (multiple valid ways to complete a task).
VLA models often face challenges in generalizing to new robots, environments, and objects in real-world scenarios.
results: CogACT achieved a 61.3% success rate on variant aggregation tasks and 74.8% on visual matching tasks in SIMPLER simulation with Google Robot, outperforming RT-2-X (54.4% and 46.3%).

In real-world tests, CogACT demonstrated superior performance, including a 71.2% success rate on seen environments and 61.5% on unseen environments with the Realman robot, and 61.4% with unseen objects on the Franka robot.
The research showed that scaling up the specialized action module, even modestly, leads to significant performance improvements, with the large action module achieving a 65.7% average success rate.
solution: CogACT introduces a componentized Vision-Language-Action (VLA) architecture that separates task understanding (cognition) from execution (action) through distinct vision, language, and action modules.

It employs a specialized action module based on a diffusion transformer (DiT) that directly models continuous and multimodal robotic action distributions, conditioned by cognitive features from the VLM.
The model integrates an adaptive action ensemble algorithm during inference to generate and combine multiple action predictions, enhancing robustness and smoothing action trajectories.
paper_summary.summary: CogACT is a Vision-Language-Action model that improves robotic manipulation by adopting a componentized architecture and a specialized diffusion-based action module. This approach directly models continuous and multimodal robotic actions, leading to significantly higher success rates across diverse simulation and real-world tasks compared to existing VLA models.

title: CogACT: A Foundational Vision-Language-Action Model for Synergizing Cognition and Action in Robotic Manipulation

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models demonstrate remarkable potential for generalizable robotic manipulation. The execution of complex multi-step behaviors in VLA models can be improved by robust instruction grounding, a critical component for effective control. However, current paradigms predominantly rely on coarse, high-level task instructions during supervised fine-tuning. This instruction grounding gap leaves models without explicit intermediate guidance, leading to severe compounding errors in long-horizon tasks. Therefore, bridging this instruction gap and providing scalable post-training for VLA models is urgent. To tackle this problem, we propose \method, the first subtask-aware VLA framework integrated with a scalable offline post-training pipeline. Our framework leverages a large language model to decompose high-level demonstrations into fine-grained atomic subtasks. This approach utilizes a pretrained predictive world model to score candidate action chunks against subtask goals in the latent space, mitigating error accumulation while significantly improving long-horizon robustness. Furthermore, this approach enables highly efficient Group Relative Policy Optimization without the prohibitive expenses associated with online rollouts on physical robots. Extensive simulations validate that our AtomVLA maintains strong robustness under perturbations. When evaluated against fundamental baseline models, it achieves an average success rate of 97.0\% on the LIBERO benchmark and 48.0\% on the LIBERO-PRO benchmark. Finally, experiments conducted in the real world using the Galaxea R1 Lite platform confirm its broad applicability across diverse tasks, especially long-horizon tasks. All datasets, checkpoints, and code will be released to the public domain following the acceptance of this work for future research.

keyInsights: High robotic manipulation performance can be achieved with in-domain multi-task demonstrations, suggesting that extensive cross-embodiment pre-training is not always necessary.

Offline post-training, guided by a latent world model and fine-grained subtask instructions, consistently improves task performance by enhancing long-horizon goal alignment and robustness to perturbations.
The use of action chunking significantly improves planning capabilities and generates more stable action sequences, with a chunk size of 4 empirically yielding optimal manipulation performance.
originalProblem: Existing VLA models often face an "instruction grounding gap," providing insufficient intermediate guidance for complex multi-step tasks, leading to error accumulation.

Scalable post-training is challenging due to the high computational cost and safety concerns of online reinforcement learning, alongside limitations of pixel-level generative world models that can suffer from autoregressive error accumulation.
Many VLA architectures require substantial computational resources and vast datasets for pre-training, hindering their practical deployment in resource-constrained environments.
results: Achieved an average success rate of 97.0% on the LIBERO benchmark and 48.0% on the challenging LIBERO-PRO benchmark, surpassing various baseline models.
Post-training contributed a 4.0% increase in average success rate over the SFT baseline on LIBERO, with a notable 6.0% gain on LIBERO-Goal tasks.

Demonstrated robust real-world performance on the Galaxea R1 Lite platform, achieving a 47.5% average success rate under generalization settings compared to 29.2% for the baseline π0, particularly excelling in deformable object manipulation.
solution: Utilizes a Large Language Model (GPT-4o) to decompose high-level task instructions into fine-grained "atomic subtasks," providing explicit, stage-wise guidance for the robotic policy.

Employs a predictive latent world model built on a frozen V-JEPA2 visual encoder to forecast future latent states, generating reliable reward signals for candidate actions during scalable offline post-training.

Applies Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to refine the VLA model's action head, using rewards from the latent world model and a KL penalty against a frozen SFT policy for efficient and stable policy improvement.
paper_summary.summary: AtomVLA introduces a two-stage post-training framework that enhances robotic manipulation by decomposing high-level instructions into fine-grained subtasks using an LLM and employing a predictive latent world model for scalable offline policy refinement. This approach achieved an average success rate of 97.0% on the LIBERO benchmark and 48.0% on LIBERO-PRO, demonstrating improved robustness and generalization across complex tasks.

title: AtomVLA: Scalable Post-Training for Robotic Manipulation via Predictive Latent World Models

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models demonstrate remarkable potential for generalizable robotic manipulation. The performance of VLA models can be improved by integrating with action chunking, a critical technique for effective control. However, action chunking linearly scales up action dimensions in VLA models with increased chunking sizes. This reduces the inference efficiency. To tackle this problem, we propose PD-VLA, the first parallel decoding framework for VLA models integrated with action chunking. Our framework reformulates autoregressive decoding as a nonlinear system solved by parallel fixed-point iterations. This approach preserves model performance with mathematical guarantees while significantly improving decoding speed. In addition, it enables training-free acceleration without architectural changes, as well as seamless synergy with existing acceleration techniques. Extensive simulations validate that our PD-VLA maintains competitive success rates while achieving 2.52 times execution frequency on manipulators (with 7 degrees of freedom) compared with the fundamental VLA model. Furthermore, we experimentally identify the most effective settings for acceleration. Finally, real-world experiments validate its high applicability across different tasks.

organization_info.name: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: Parallel fixed-point iteration can efficiently transform sequential autoregressive generation into a faster, iterative process, providing substantial speedup without modifying the underlying VLA model architecture.

The inference bottleneck introduced by action chunking's increased action dimensionality is effectively mitigated by parallel decoding, allowing VLA models to maintain performance while achieving higher control frequencies.
The phenomenon of "fixed tokens" (correctly predicted tokens early in the parallel decoding process) contributes to faster convergence, particularly for low-entropy actions like gripper states.
originalProblem: Action chunking, a technique used to improve VLA model performance and consistency in robotic manipulation, linearly increases the dimensionality of action sequences.

Standard autoregressive decoding predicts each action token sequentially, leading to inference times that scale linearly with increased action dimensions, thereby limiting real-time control frequencies.
Existing acceleration methods for VLA models often require re-training or architectural modifications, which can be computationally expensive and limit deployment flexibility.
results: The framework achieved a 2.52x increase in execution frequency (4.56 Hz vs. 1.81 Hz) for VLA models with action chunking on the CALVIN benchmark.
PD-VLA demonstrated competitive manipulation performance, achieving a 94.7% overall average success rate on the LIBERO benchmark and 94.1% for the 1/5 subtask on CALVIN.
Real-world robotic experiments showed PD-VLA consistently outperforming standard LLaVA-VLA, with an 80% success rate for "push button" (vs. 60%) and 60% for "pour water" (vs. 10%).
solution: PD-VLA develops a training-free parallel decoding mechanism that integrates into pre-trained VLA models without architectural changes or additional training.

It re-frames autoregressive decoding by replacing the causal attention mechanism with a bidirectional attention mechanism within the LLM, enabling simultaneous updates of all action tokens in each iteration.
The approach leverages Jacobi decoding principles, iteratively predicting action tokens until a fixed point is reached, which typically requires fewer passes than sequential autoregressive decoding.
paper_summary.summary: PD-VLA introduces a training-free parallel decoding framework to accelerate Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, particularly those using action chunking, by reformulating autoregressive decoding into fixed-point iterations. This approach boosts VLA model execution frequency by 2.52x and achieves higher success rates in real-world robotic manipulation tasks compared to standard autoregressive methods.

title: PD-VLA: Accelerating Vision-Language-Action Model Integrated with Action Chunking via Parallel Decoding

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are experiencing rapid development and demonstrating promising capabilities in robotic manipulation tasks. However, scaling up VLA models presents several critical challenges: (1) Training new VLA models from scratch demands substantial computational resources and extensive datasets. Given the current scarcity of robot data, it becomes particularly valuable to fully leverage well-pretrained VLA model weights during the scaling process. (2) Real-time control requires carefully balancing model capacity with computational efficiency. To address these challenges, We propose AdaMoE, a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture that inherits pretrained weights from dense VLA models, and scales up the action expert by substituting the feedforward layers into sparsely activated MoE layers. AdaMoE employs a decoupling technique that decouples expert selection from expert weighting through an independent scale adapter working alongside the traditional router. This enables experts to be selected based on task relevance while contributing with independently controlled weights, allowing collaborative expert utilization rather than winner-takes-all dynamics. Our approach demonstrates that expertise need not monopolize. Instead, through collaborative expert utilization, we can achieve superior performance while maintaining computational efficiency. AdaMoE consistently outperforms the baseline model across key benchmarks, delivering performance gains of 1.8% on LIBERO and 9.3% on RoboTwin. Most importantly, a substantial 21.5% improvement in real-world experiments validates its practical effectiveness for robotic manipulation tasks.

keyInsights: Decoupling expert selection from expert weighting in MoE resolves the conflict between load balancing and task-specific specialization, enabling more nuanced expert collaboration.
The router in MoE can introduce valuable inductive biases for action generation, as even a collapsed MoE can outperform dense models.
Optimal performance in specialized MoE for VLA depends critically on the top-k selection, number of experts, and a carefully tuned load balancing loss weight.
originalProblem: Scaling Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models is computationally expensive and demands extensive, high-quality robot data, which is scarce.
Balancing high model capacity for complex robotic tasks with computational efficiency for real-time control is challenging.
Traditional Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures face conflicting optimization objectives in VLA, where load balancing often hinders task-specific performance.
results: AdaMoE improved average success rates by 1.8% on LIBERO (to 96.0%) and by 9.3% on RoboTwin 2.0 (to 49.7%) compared to the \u03c00 baseline.

In real-world robotic experiments, AdaMoE achieved an average success rate of 71.5%, a 21.5% improvement over the 
\u03c00 baseline, particularly on complex tasks.
Analysis of expert activation patterns revealed clear task-dependent specialization, with different experts correlating to specific manipulation phases.
solution: Introduces AdaMoE, an action-specialized MoE architecture integrated into an existing VLA model's action expert.
Proposes a novel decoupled design where expert selection is handled by a router, and expert weighting is independently adjusted by a separate scale adapter.
Initializes the MoE architecture by inheriting pre-trained weights from a dense VLA model, enabling efficient scaling with low additional training cost.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University and collaborators developed AdaMoE, an action-specialized Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models in robotic manipulation. It achieves an average success rate of 96.0% on LIBERO, a 9.3% performance gain on RoboTwin 2.0, and a 21.5% improvement in real-world robotic tasks compared to baselines by decoupling expert selection and weighting.

title: Expertise need not monopolize: Action-Specialized Mixture of Experts for Vision-Language-Action Learning

abstract: Most existing vision-language-action (VLA) models for robotic manipulation lack progress awareness, typically relying on hand-crafted heuristics for task termination. This limitation is particularly severe in long-horizon tasks involving cascaded sub-goals. In this work, we investigate the estimation and integration of task progress, proposing a novel model named {\textbf \vla}. Our technical contributions are twofold: (1) \emph{robust progress estimation}: We pre-train a progress estimator on large-scale, unsupervised video-text robotic datasets. This estimator achieves a low prediction residual (0.07 on a scale of $[0, 1]$) in simulation and demonstrates zero-shot generalization to unseen real-world samples, and (2) \emph{differentiable progress guidance}: We introduce an inverse dynamics world model that maps predicted action tokens into future latent visual states. These latents are then processed by the progress estimator; by applying a maximal progress regularization, we establish a differentiable pipeline that provides progress-piloted guidance to refine action tokens. Extensive experiments on the CALVIN and LIBERO benchmarks, alongside real-world robot deployment, consistently demonstrate substantial improvements in success rates and generalization over strong baselines.

keyInsights: Explicitly integrating a differentiable task progress signal directly into the action generation process significantly enhances the goal-directedness and efficiency of diffusion policies for robotic manipulation.
Pretraining the progress estimator and world model on large-scale datasets with robust visual features is crucial for achieving strong generalization to unseen appearance shifts and novel objects.

Online RL finetuning, incorporating progress-specific regularization like a monotonicity loss, further improves the alignment of the learned progress estimator and the robustness of the guided policy to real-world execution variability.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often lack an explicit, dense understanding of task progress, leading to reliance on implicit cues or brittle, hand-crafted termination heuristics.

Generative policies, such as diffusion models, may produce visually plausible actions but often fail to efficiently advance towards the specified task goal, particularly in complex, long-horizon tasks.
Learning robust task progress directly from raw visual observations is challenging due to nuisance variations, which limits generalization to new environments or object instances.
results: ProgressVLA achieved a 95.2% 1-in-a-row success rate and an average completed length of 3.73 on the CALVIN benchmark, demonstrating improved performance over unguided policies and baselines.

The full ProgressVLA model attained an average success rate of 84.5% on the LIBERO benchmark, showing a substantial improvement on the LIBERO-LONG split (66.2%) compared to baselines like OpenVLA (53.7%).

In real-world robot experiments, ProgressVLA with guidance achieved an average 76% success rate, reducing end-effector travel distance by 35% and execution steps by 72% compared to Octo, indicating increased efficiency and reliability.
solution: A robust progress estimator is developed, utilizing frozen DINOv2 and CLIP features, to output a normalized scalar progress score (0-1) conditioned on language instructions and current/initial visual observations.

A two-stage diffusion policy, operating in a latent action space, is guided at inference time by gradients derived from the differentiable progress estimator via an action-conditioned world model, steering action sampling towards maximizing progress.

The framework incorporates online Reinforcement Learning (RL) finetuning, employing a margin-based monotonicity loss for the progress estimator and a KL-regularized objective to refine the diffusion policy based on real-world experience.
paper_summary.summary: ProgressVLA introduces a framework for robotic manipulation that integrates an explicit, differentiable task progress estimator to guide diffusion policies. This approach enables Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to generate more goal-directed and efficient trajectories, resulting in improved success rates and reduced execution steps across complex, long-horizon tasks in benchmarks and real-world deployments.

title: ProgressVLA: Progress-Guided Diffusion Policy for Vision-Language Robotic Manipulation

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated strong performance in robotic manipulation, yet their closed-loop deployment is hindered by the high latency and compute cost of repeatedly running large vision-language backbones at every timestep. We observe that VLA inference exhibits structured redundancies across temporal, spatial, and depth dimensions, and that most existing efficiency methods ignore action context, despite its central role in embodied tasks. To address this gap, we propose Action-Context-aware Adaptive Computation for VLA models (AC^2-VLA), a unified framework that conditions computation on current visual observations, language instructions, and previous action states. Based on this action-centric context, AC^2-VLA adaptively performs cognition reuse across timesteps, token pruning, and selective execution of model components within a unified mechanism. To train the adaptive policy, we introduce an action-guided self-distillation scheme that preserves the behavior of the dense VLA policy while enabling structured sparsification that transfers across tasks and settings. Extensive experiments on robotic manipulation benchmarks show that AC^2-VLA achieves up to a 1.79\times speedup while reducing FLOPs to 29.4% of the dense baseline, with comparable task success.

keyInsights: VLA inference contains structured redundancies (temporal, spatial, depth-wise) that can be exploited for computational efficiency.
The robot's "action context" (previous action state, current manipulation intent) is a critical, underutilized signal for adaptively allocating computational resources.
A unified framework that coordinates temporal reuse, spatial token pruning, and depth-wise layer skipping, guided by action context, yields superior efficiency compared to isolated approaches.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art VLA models for robotic manipulation incur high computational costs and inference latency, hindering real-time, closed-loop deployment.
Large multimodal backbones are executed repeatedly at every control step, reducing control frequency and responsiveness in dynamic environments.
Existing efficiency strategies often base compute allocation solely on visual cues, which don't always correlate with actual control difficulty or task-relevant reasoning.
results: AC^2-VLA achieved up to a 1.79× wall-clock speedup and reduced FLOPs to 29.4% of the dense CogACT baseline.
The framework improved the average task success rate to 76.8% on Google Robot Visual Matching, exceeding the dense baseline (74.8%) and other state-of-the-art VLA models.

It consistently outperformed other efficiency methods across benchmarks, demonstrating a superior speed-accuracy trade-off, and ablation studies confirmed the synergistic benefits of its unified components, particularly improved robustness via temporal consistency from cache reuse.
solution: An "action-prior router" is introduced to dynamically allocate computation by predicting unified gates for efficiency mechanisms.
The router utilizes an "action-centric context," fusing the previous action state, vision features, and language instruction, as the primary signal for compute allocation.

The framework unifies three complementary efficiency strategies: cognition caching (temporal reuse), action-context-aware token pruning (spatial sparsification), and conditional layer skipping (depth-wise execution).
paper_summary.summary: AC^2-VLA is an action-context-aware adaptive computation framework designed for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to enhance efficiency in robotic manipulation. It achieves up to a 1.79× wall-clock speedup and reduces FLOPs to 29.4% of the dense baseline, improving task success rates to 76.8% on Google Robot Visual Matching while coordinating temporal reuse, token pruning, and layer skipping.

title: AC^2-VLA: Action-Context-Aware Adaptive Computation in Vision-Language-Action Models for Efficient Robotic Manipulation

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have achieved remarkable breakthroughs in robotics, with the action chunk playing a dominant role in these advances. Given the real-time and continuous nature of robotic motion control, the strategies for fusing a queue of successive action chunks have a profound impact on the overall performance of VLA models. Existing methods suffer from jitter, stalling, or even pauses in robotic action execution, which not only limits the achievable execution speed but also reduces the overall success rate of task completion. This paper introduces VLA-RAIL (A Real-Time Asynchronous Inference Linker), a novel framework designed to address these issues by conducting model inference and robot motion control asynchronously and guaranteeing smooth, continuous, and high-speed action execution. The core contributions of the paper are two fold: a Trajectory Smoother that effectively filters out the noise and jitter in the trajectory of one action chunk using polynomial fitting and a Chunk Fuser that seamlessly align the current executing trajectory and the newly arrived chunk, ensuring position, velocity, and acceleration continuity between two successive action chunks. We validate the effectiveness of VLA-RAIL on a benchmark of dynamic simulation tasks and several real-world manipulation tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that VLA-RAIL significantly reduces motion jitter, enhances execution speed, and improves task success rates, which will become a key infrastructure for the large-scale deployment of VLA models.

organization_info.name: China Mobile (Hangzhou) Information Technology Co., Ltd.

keyInsights: Asynchronously decoupling VLA model inference from real-time robot control is critical for overcoming latency challenges and achieving smooth, high-speed execution.
Achieving C2 (position, velocity, acceleration) continuity between independently inferred action chunks through dual-quintic spline interpolation is essential for stable and precise robot motion.

Sophisticated trajectory post-processing can significantly enhance the real-world performance of existing VLA models, including task success rates and execution speed, without necessitating architectural modifications or retraining.
originalProblem: VLA models produce robot actions with high-frequency oscillations and abrupt transitions between successive chunks, leading to jerky, unstable motion and reduced control accuracy.
Synchronous execution of VLA models results in inefficient "pause-and-go" behavior, hindering real-time performance and task completion speed.
Current VLA model deployments are tightly coupled to specific hardware and software, limiting scalability and cross-platform portability across diverse robotic systems.
results: VLA-RAIL consistently produced trajectories with near-zero standard deviation for accelerations, ensuring C2 continuity and eliminating both intra-chunk noise and inter-chunk discontinuities.
The framework achieved a 2.09x speedup in a bottle-grasping task, completing it in 9.07 seconds compared to 18.93 seconds for raw VLA outputs.
VLA-RAIL improved the success rates of five different VLA models in a bottle pick-and-place task by absolute gains ranging from 0.10 to 0.725, with top-performing models reaching a 0.95 success rate.
solution: A decoupled client-server architecture utilizes ZMQ communication and unified interfaces to enable plug-and-play integration of various VLA models with heterogeneous robotic platforms.
A two-stage action chunk post-processing pipeline applies intra-chunk cubic polynomial smoothing to filter noise and inter-chunk dual-quintic spline interpolation for C2 continuity.
An execution acceleration strategy allows robots to operate at speeds beyond the VLA model's training data frequency by leveraging continuous trajectories, without requiring model retraining.
paper_summary.summary: VLA-RAIL is an asynchronous inference linker that enables Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to achieve smooth, C2-continuous motion on real robots, accelerating task execution and enhancing success rates without requiring model retraining. Developed by China Mobile (Hangzhou) Information Technology Co., Ltd., it addresses real-time deployment challenges for heterogeneous robotic platforms.

title: VLA-RAIL: A Real-Time Asynchronous Inference Linker for VLA Models and Robots

abstract: We propose a standalone autoregressive (AR) Action Expert that generates actions as a continuous causal sequence while conditioning on refreshable vision-language prefixes. In contrast to existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models and diffusion policies that reset temporal context with each new observation and predict actions reactively, our Action Expert maintains its own history through a long-lived memory and is inherently context-aware. This structure addresses the frequency mismatch between fast control and slow reasoning, enabling efficient independent pretraining of kinematic syntax and modular integration with heavy perception backbones, naturally ensuring spatio-temporally consistent action generation across frames. To synchronize these asynchronous hybrid V-L-A modalities, we utilize a re-anchoring mechanism that mathematically accounts for perception staleness during both training and inference. Experiments on simulated and real-robot manipulation tasks demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively replace traditional chunk-based action heads for both specialist and generalist policies. AR-VLA exhibits superior history awareness and substantially smoother action trajectories while maintaining or exceeding the task success rates of state-of-the-art reactive VLAs. Overall, our work introduces a scalable, context-aware action generation schema that provides a robust structural foundation for training effective robotic policies.

keyInsights: A truly autoregressive action expert, maintaining a persistent internal state of its own trajectory, naturally achieves context-awareness and generates smoother, more consistent robot control.

Decoupling the high-frequency motor control thread from the slower VLM perception thread allows for asynchronous operation, effectively addressing the fundamental frequency mismatch and improving overall efficiency.
Pretraining the action expert on kinematic data alone enables it to learn the 'syntax of movement' efficiently before multimodal alignment, leading to faster convergence and improved performance.
originalProblem: Existing VLA models and diffusion policies operate reactively, predicting actions in isolated 'chunks' and discarding temporal context, which leads to 'Markovian amnesia' and jerky robot control.
There is a frequency mismatch between slow, high-level semantic reasoning from VLMs and the high-frequency demands of precise motor control, causing performance bottlenecks.
Current robot policies often lack a persistent internal state of their action history, hindering performance in tasks requiring temporal awareness or memory of unobservable states.
results: AR-VLA achieved an average 61.5% success rate in SimplerEnv manipulation tasks, improving over CogACT (52.1%) and Pi-0-Fast (49.0%), and demonstrated an 89% average success rate in real-world zero-shot WidowX tasks.

The framework maintained a stable 29ms per action control frequency even when the perception backbone operated at a slower 70ms per frame, resulting in significantly smoother motion with the lowest maximum and average jerk.

The model substantially outperformed existing reactive policies on history-aware tasks (PushT2, Stack3) requiring memory of unobservable states, validating its enhanced temporal awareness and robustness in non-Markovian environments.
solution: AR-VLA employs a stateful, two-stage architecture featuring a standalone Autoregressive Action Expert that generates robot actions as a continuous causal sequence.

A Unified Transformer Decoder with a Hybrid Key-Value (HKV) Cache manages two context streams: a long-lived Proprioceptive Stream (FIFO) for action history and a refreshable Visual-Language Stream for semantic prefixes.

Dynamic Temporal Re-anchoring (DTR) with Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) resolves temporal misalignment between asynchronous streams, enabling generalization from short training contexts to long-horizon inference.
paper_summary.summary: AR-VLA introduces a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model featuring a truly autoregressive Action Expert that generates robot actions as a continuous causal sequence. This framework, developed by researchers at INSAIT, Sofia University and KU Leuven, utilizes a persistent kinematic history and asynchronous vision-language conditioning, leading to smoother trajectories, enhanced temporal consistency, and higher success rates (e.g., 61.5% in SimplerEnv, 89% in real-world WidowX tasks) compared to existing reactive policies, particularly in tasks requiring memory of past unobservable states.

title: AR-VLA: True Autoregressive Action Expert for Vision-Language-Action Models

abstract: Recent vision-language-action (VLA) models have significantly advanced robotic manipulation by unifying perception, reasoning, and control. To achieve such integration, recent studies adopt a predictive paradigm that models future visual states or world knowledge to guide action generation. However, these models emphasize forecasting outcomes rather than reasoning about the underlying process of change, which is essential for determining how to act. To address this, we propose $\Delta$VLA, a prior-guided framework that models world-knowledge variations relative to an explicit current-world knowledge prior for action generation, rather than regressing absolute future world states. Specifically, 1) to construct the current world knowledge prior, we propose the Prior-Guided WorldKnowledge Extractor (PWKE). It extracts manipulable regions, spatial relations, and semantic cues from the visual input, guided by auxiliary heads and prior pseudo labels, thus reducing redundancy. 2) Building upon this, to represent how world knowledge evolves under actions, we introduce the Latent World Variation Quantization (LWVQ). It learns a discrete latent space via a VQ-VAE objective to encode world knowledge variations, shifting prediction from full modalities to compact latent. 3)Moreover, to mitigate interference during variation modeling, we design the Conditional Variation Attention (CV-Atten), whichpromotes disentangled learning and preserves the independence of knowledge representations. Extensive experiments on both simulated benchmarks and real-world robotic tasks demonstrate $\Delta$VLA achieves state-of-the-art performance while improving efficiency. Code and real-world execution videos are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Modeling prior-grounded, discrete world-knowledge variations directly improves action generation, providing a more causally relevant and stable learning signal for robotic policies.
Explicitly extracting a current-world knowledge prior acts as a crucial causal anchor, helping distinguish what should change versus remain invariant under an instruction.

Disentangled learning of multimodal variations through structured attention reduces cross-modal interference, leading to more precise and robust robot control, especially in geometry-critical interactions.
originalProblem: Existing VLA models primarily predict absolute future states, overlooking the process of change and variations induced by robot actions.
A lack of an explicit current-world knowledge prior in VLA models leads to ungrounded change prediction and inaccurate attribution of action effects.
Continuous representations of world variations are unstable and inconsistent, making them unsuitable as a compact conditioning interface for policy learning.
results: Attained state-of-the-art performance on simulation benchmarks, including a 97.8% average success rate on LIBERO and 80.4% on RoboTwin 2.0 bimanual tasks.
Achieved strong real-world task success rates (72% on Galaxea R1 Lite, 69% on AgileX Cobot Magic) for complex, long-horizon tasks, surpassing predictive baselines.
Demonstrated superior efficiency with 0.105 seconds inference latency, 76.2 Hz throughput, and significantly reduced training cost (4.9 hours per 10k steps) compared to previous VLA models.
solution: The ∆VLA framework models discrete world-knowledge variations relative to an explicit current-world knowledge prior for action generation.
A Prior-Guided World Knowledge Extractor (PWKE) constructs the current-world knowledge ($W_t$) using pre-trained vision models and auxiliary supervision for manipulable regions, depth, and semantics.

Latent World Variation Quantization (LWVQ) encodes action-induced world variations into a discrete latent space using a VQ-VAE, providing a stable policy conditioning interface, while Conditional Variation Attention (CV-Atten) integrates these variations into an LLM, preventing cross-modal interference.
paper_summary.summary: A new framework, ∆VLA, enhances Vision-Language-Action models for robotic manipulation by explicitly modeling prior-grounded, discrete world-knowledge variations rather than absolute future states. It achieved a 97.8% average success rate on the LIBERO benchmark and 72% on real-world tasks with Galaxea R1 Lite, while also demonstrating higher efficiency with 0.105 seconds latency.

title: $Δ$VLA: Prior-Guided Vision-Language-Action Models via World Knowledge Variation

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated significant advantages in robotic manipulation. However, their reliance on vision and language often leads to suboptimal performance in tasks involving visual occlusion, fine-grained manipulation, and physical contact. To address these challenges, we propose TacVLA, a fine-tuned VLA model by incorporating tactile modalities into the transformer-based policy to enhance fine-grained manipulation capabilities. Specifically, we introduce a contact-aware gating mechanism that selectively activates tactile tokens only when contact is detected, enabling adaptive multimodal fusion while avoiding irrelevant tactile interference. The fused visual, language, and tactile tokens are jointly processed within the transformer architecture to strengthen cross-modal grounding during contact-rich interaction. Extensive experiments on constraint-locked disassembly, in-box picking and robustness evaluations demonstrate that our model outperforms baselines, improving the performance by averaging 20% success rate in disassembly, 60% in in-box picking and 2.1x improvement in scenarios with visual occlusion. Videos are available at this https URL and code will be released.

keyInsights: Efficient and effective integration of tactile sensing into VLA models, particularly through a compact tokenization scheme, can substantially enhance robotic manipulation capabilities in visually challenging and contact-rich environments.

A contact-aware gating mechanism for multimodal fusion is critical for robust performance, as it selectively leverages tactile information only when physically relevant, preventing performance degradation from uninformative tactile signals during non-contact phases.

Adaptive fusion of vision, language, and touch enables robots to achieve higher success rates and greater resilience to visual occlusions and dynamic disturbances in complex, fine-grained manipulation tasks.
originalProblem: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models rely heavily on visual and linguistic information, leading to failures in scenarios with severe visual occlusions or when fine-grained physical properties like contact forces are critical.

Existing approaches for integrating tactile sensing into VLA often treat tactile observations as dense image-like inputs, increasing computational costs, and naive concatenation can result in inefficient cross-modal interaction during non-contact phases.
VLA systems typically lack robustness to unexpected physical resistance, dynamic contact variations, or external disturbances due to the absence of direct physical interaction feedback.
results: TacVLA achieved an average success rate of 83.75% across four constraint-locked disassembly tasks, outperforming the finetuned Pi0.5 baseline (63.75%) and diffusion-based policies (48.75%).
In the visually challenging in-box picking task, TacVLA demonstrated a 70% success rate, a notable improvement over the finetuned Pi0.5 baseline (10%) and other diffusion policies (0-5%).

The system showed enhanced robustness under severe visual occlusion, maintaining over 60% average success (compared to ~30% for the vision-only baseline) and successfully recovering from unexpected human disturbances.
solution: TacVLA integrates a compact tactile array into a transformer-based VLA policy, utilizing a lightweight Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)-based encoder to project raw tactile maps into low-dimensional tactile tokens.

A contact-aware gating module adaptively activates tactile tokens only when physical contact is detected, preventing interference from uninformative tactile data during non-contact phases while maintaining a fixed token structure.

The system strengthens cross-modal grounding across vision, language, and touch within a pretrained Vision-Language Model (VLM) backbone, with an action expert module predicting continuous action sequences conditioned on the fused multimodal tokens.
