paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Purdue University developed TacVLA, a system that enhances Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by integrating tactile sensing with a contact-aware gating mechanism. This approach achieved an 83.75% average success rate on complex disassembly tasks and 70% on in-box picking, showing improved robustness in visually occluded or human-perturbed environments.

title: TacVLA: Contact-Aware Tactile Fusion for Robust Vision-Language-Action Manipulation

abstract: Action chunking has recently emerged as a standard practice in flow-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. However, the effect and choice of the execution horizon - the number of actions to be executed from each predicted chunk - remains underexplored. In this work, we first show that varying the execution horizon leads to substantial performance deviations, with performance initially improving and then declining as the horizon increases. To uncover the reasons, we analyze the cross- and self-attention weights in flow-based VLAs and reveal two key phenomena: (i) intra-chunk actions attend invariantly to vision-language tokens, limiting adaptability to environmental changes; and (ii) the initial and terminal action tokens serve as stable anchors, forming latent centers around which intermediate actions are organized. Motivated by these insights, we interpret action self-attention weights as a proxy for the model's predictive limit and propose AutoHorizon, the first test-time method that dynamically estimates the execution horizon for each predicted action chunk to adapt to changing perceptual conditions. Across simulated and real-world robotic manipulation tasks, AutoHorizon is performant, incurs negligible computational overhead, and generalizes across diverse tasks and flow-based models.

keyInsights: An optimal execution horizon exists for VLA policies, with performance exhibiting a unimodal (peaked) relationship, demonstrating a trade-off between long-term consistency and short-term reactivity.
VLA intra-chunk actions exhibit invariant attention to vision-language tokens, limiting their adaptive capabilities to dynamic environmental changes over time.

Action self-attention maps reveal 'radial action sinks' at the initial and terminal actions of a chunk, indicating intermediate actions are organized around these stable anchors, and declining attention signifies increasing error amplification.
originalProblem: Fixed execution horizons in VLA action chunking policies are suboptimal, leading to significant performance fluctuations that often require costly brute-force evaluation or human heuristics.
The optimal balance between long-term temporal consistency and short-term reactivity varies dynamically throughout a task, necessitating an adaptive approach.
Prior works lack a principled and efficient method to dynamically determine the execution horizon, leaving this crucial aspect underexplored despite its impact on performance.
results: AutoHorizon consistently outperformed all baselines, including 'Static Oracle+', on the LIBERO and RoboTwin benchmarks for both "pi"0.5 and GR00T N1.5 models, achieving success rates up to 98.0% on LIB-Object.

In real-world experiments on a Franka robot, AutoHorizon achieved the highest success rates across three pick-and-place tasks (e.g., 99.0% for 'put Rubik's cube into bowl') compared to fixed-horizon policies.

The method demonstrates robustness to hyperparameter changes, generates smoother joint velocity transitions, and incurs negligible computational overhead, making it practical for real-time adaptive robotic control.
solution: Conducted an in-depth analysis of VLA attention mechanisms, revealing 'invariant attention to vision-language tokens' and 'radial action sinks' within self-attention maps.
Proposed AutoHorizon, a test-time algorithm that interprets action self-attention weights as implicit indicators of the model's predictive limit.

The AutoHorizon algorithm dynamically estimates the execution horizon by using a bidirectional soft-pointer mechanism on entropy-filtered attention maps to identify the point where the attention mass plateaus, marking the boundary of reliable prediction.
paper_summary.summary: This work introduces AutoHorizon, a test-time algorithm that dynamically determines the optimal execution horizon for action chunking in flow-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. By analyzing VLA attention patterns, the method significantly improves robotic manipulation success rates through adaptive balancing of temporal consistency and reactivity.

title: VLA Knows Its Limits

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer a pivotal approach to learning robotic manipulation at scale by repurposing large pre-trained Vision-Language-Models (VLM) to output robotic actions. However, adapting VLMs for robotic domains comes with an unnecessarily high computational cost, which we attribute to the tokenization scheme of visual inputs. In this work, we aim to enable efficient VLA training by proposing Oat-VLA, an Object-Agent-centric Tokenization for VLAs. Building on the insights of object-centric representation learning, our method introduces an inductive bias towards scene objects and the agent's own visual information. As a result, we find that Oat-VLA can drastically reduce the number of visual tokens to just a few tokens without sacrificing performance. We reveal that Oat-VLA converges at least twice as fast as OpenVLA on the LIBERO suite, as well as outperform OpenVLA in diverse real-world pick and place tasks.

keyInsights: Drastically reducing visual tokens from 256 to 16 (a 93.75% decrease) significantly enhances VLA training efficiency, enabling faster convergence and larger batch sizes.
Introducing an inductive bias by explicitly focusing on objects and the agent's gripper provides more semantically relevant visual input, improving learning efficacy for manipulation tasks.
The modular design of Oat-VLA allows it to leverage existing VLA checkpoints and their pre-trained knowledge, modifying only the visual tokenization frontend without altering the LLM backbone.
originalProblem: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models incur high computational costs due to dense patch-based visual tokenization, which processes an excessive number of visual tokens.
The substantial compute requirements for VLA training limit accessibility for many research groups, hindering broader participation and progress in the field.
Current VLA visual processing often includes redundant background information, leading to inefficiencies as not all visual input is equally relevant for robotic manipulation tasks.
results: Oat-VLA achieves more than a 2x speedup in VLA training time and steps compared to OpenVLA, processing over twice as many examples per second due to reduced memory requirements and larger batch sizes.

The method consistently matches or slightly outperforms OpenVLA across all four LIBERO benchmark task suites and shows a significant performance advantage (52% vs. 72% in-distribution, 29% vs. 46% out-of-distribution) in real-world pick and place tasks.

Qualitative assessments and quantitative results on real-world tasks indicate that Oat-VLA enables greater precision in object manipulation, with fewer mis-grasps or incorrect placements compared to the baseline.
solution: Oat-VLA implements a novel visual tokenization scheme that selectively extracts object-centric and agent-centric visual information instead of using dense image patches.
Unsupervised object extraction (FT-Dinosaur) is used to generate a fixed number of object masks, with average pooling applied to corresponding visual patches to create compact object-centric tokens.

A dedicated gripper detector identifies the robot's end-effector, and a fixed grid of high-resolution visual patches is selected around this location to form agent-centric tokens, preserving crucial manipulation details.
paper_summary.summary: Oat-VLA introduces an object-agent-centric tokenization method for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, reducing visual tokens by 93.75% and achieving a 2x training speedup. This approach maintains or improves performance on robotic manipulation benchmarks and demonstrates increased robustness in real-world tasks.

title: Focusing on What Matters: Object-Agent-centric Tokenization for Vision Language Action models

abstract: A central challenge in mobile manipulation is preserving multiple plausible action models while remaining reactive during execution. A bottle in a cluttered scene can often be approached and grasped in multiple valid ways. Robust behavior depends on preserving this action diversity while remaining reactive as the scene evolves. Diffusion policies are appealing because they model multimodal action distributions rather than collapsing to one solution. But in practice, full iterative denoising is costly at control time. Action chunking helps amortize inference, yet it also creates partially open-loop behavior, allowing small mismatches to accumulate into drift. We present AnchorVLA, a diffusion-based VLA policy for mobile manipulation built on the core insight that when sampling begins near a plausible solution manifold, extensive denoising is unnecessary to recover multimodal, valid actions. AnchorVLA combines a lightweight VLA adaptation backbone with an anchored diffusion action head, which denoises locally around anchor trajectories using a truncated diffusion schedule. This retains multimodal action generation while reducing inference cost for closed-loop control. Crucially, to mitigate chunking-induced drift, we introduce a test-time self-correction mechanism via a lightweight residual correction module that makes high-frequency, per-step adjustments during rollout. Across diverse mobile manipulation tasks, AnchorVLA improves success and stability under disturbances and distribution shifts while maintaining low-latency inference. The source code is made available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Anchored truncated diffusion efficiently captures multimodal action distributions by localizing the denoising process around a vocabulary of expert motion patterns, drastically reducing the required diffusion steps.
A learned scoring head, integrated into the diffusion process, effectively selects the most contextually relevant and physically plausible action chunk from multiple denoised proposals.

A compact, separately trained residual correction module provides real-time, per-step micro-adjustments to planned actions, robustly counteracting accumulated errors from action chunking with minimal computational overhead.
originalProblem: Deterministic L1 regression policies commonly suffer from mode-averaging, producing physically infeasible actions in multimodal mobile manipulation tasks.
Diffusion-based generative policies, while capable of modeling complex action distributions, incur high computational costs and inference latency, hindering real-time robot control.
Action chunking, used to improve control throughput, introduces partially open-loop behavior, leading to error accumulation and trajectory drift over extended execution horizons.
results: AnchorVLA achieved an average 64.0% success rate across six mobile manipulation tasks in simulation, an 8.4% absolute improvement over the strongest baseline, AC-DiT (55.6%).
Ablation studies showed that anchored diffusion provided a 1.48x inference speedup and required 5x fewer denoising steps to achieve comparable performance to a non-anchored baseline.

The method demonstrated substantial computational savings, reducing per-episode compute by approximately 80% when using longer action chunks (H=5 vs. H=1), and achieved a 40% average success rate in real-world deployment on a Unitree Go2 Edu quadruped, outperforming SmolVLA-0.5B (15%).
solution: A lightweight VLA adaptation backbone extracts a compact latent context from multimodal observations and language instructions.

An anchored truncated diffusion action head generates multimodal action chunks by perturbing precomputed anchor trajectories with noise and denoising over a truncated schedule, then selecting the best chunk using a learned confidence score.
A lightweight residual correction module, a per-step MLP, predicts micro-adjustments to the planned macro-actions during execution to mitigate open-loop drift.
paper_summary.summary: AnchorVLA, a diffusion-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policy, enhances mobile manipulation by employing anchored truncated diffusion for efficient multimodal action generation and a residual correction module to mitigate execution drift. This approach achieved an average 64.0% success rate in simulation, outperforming strong baselines by 8.4%, and showed robust real-world performance with a 1.48x inference speedup and 80% reduction in compute.

title: AnchorVLA: Anchored Diffusion for Efficient End-to-End Mobile Manipulation

abstract: Robotic manipulation with Vision-Language-Action models requires efficient inference over long-horizon multi-modal context, where attention to dense visual tokens dominates computational cost. Existing methods optimize inference speed by reducing visual redundancy within VLA models, but they overlook the varying redundancy across robotic manipulation stages. We observe that the visual token redundancy is higher in coarse manipulation phase than in fine-grained operations, and is strongly correlated with the action dynamic. Motivated by this observation, we propose \textbf{A}ction-aware \textbf{D}ynamic \textbf{P}runing (\textbf{ADP}), a multi-modal pruning framework that integrates text-driven token selection with action-aware trajectory gating. Our method introduces a gating mechanism that conditions the pruning signal on recent action trajectories, using past motion windows to adaptively adjust token retention ratios in accordance with dynamics, thereby balancing computational efficiency and perceptual precision across different manipulation stages. Extensive experiments on the LIBERO suites and diverse real-world scenarios demonstrate that our method significantly reduces FLOPs and action inference latency (\textit{e.g.} $1.35 \times$ speed up on OpenVLA-OFT) while maintaining competitive success rates (\textit{e.g.} 25.8\% improvements with OpenVLA) compared to baselines, thereby providing a simple plug-in path to efficient robot policies that advances the efficiency and performance frontier of robotic manipulation. Our project website is: \href{this https URL}{this http URL}.

keyInsights: Visual redundancy in robotic manipulation tasks is action-aware and varies dynamically with the robot's motion, allowing for adaptive pruning that balances efficiency and precision.
Early Transformer layers (e.g., Layer 0) provide more stable and globally discriminative text-to-vision attention signals for robust visual token importance scoring than deeper layers.

A dynamic, state-aware pruning strategy that considers robot actions is crucial for efficiency gains without sacrificing accuracy, significantly outperforming static or periodically switching pruning methods.
originalProblem: High computational cost, memory footprint, and inference latency of VLA models due to processing dense visual tokens from multiple high-resolution camera views.
Existing static pruning methods are suboptimal, either over-pruning critical information or under-pruning, failing to adapt to varying visual needs across different manipulation stages.
The challenge of deploying powerful VLA models in real-world robotic systems that require real-time control and operate under computational resource constraints.
results: Achieved up to 1.35x speedup and significant FLOPs reduction on the LIBERO simulation benchmark while maintaining 94.4-96.3% average success rates.
Demonstrated a 1.49x inference speedup (reducing latency from 76.9ms to 51.8ms) and an improved average success rate from 85.8% to 88.3% on real-world Jaco2 robot tasks.
ADP with 50% visual token retention provided a 25.8% relative improvement in success rate compared to the original OpenVLA model (non-OFT version) on the LIBERO benchmark.
solution: Text-driven Anticipatory Pruning selects task-relevant visual tokens by leveraging cross-modal attention between text instructions and visual features from an early Transformer layer (layer 0).

An Action-aware Dynamic Strategy dynamically activates or deactivates pruning based on the robot's end-effector movement magnitude within action windows, adapting to coarse or fine manipulation phases.

Incorporates mitigation mechanisms like a cold start (full vision for initial steps) and pruning reset (forcing full vision after consecutive pruned windows) to prevent initial errors and prolonged information loss.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Sydney developed Action-aware Dynamic Pruning (ADP), a framework for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models that dynamically prunes visual tokens based on task instructions and robot motion. ADP reduces inference latency by up to 1.49x in real-world settings while maintaining or improving task success rates in robotic manipulation.

title: Action-aware Dynamic Pruning for Efficient Vision-Language-Action Manipulation

abstract: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) achieve strong performance in general robotic manipulation tasks by scaling imitation learning. However, existing VLAs are limited to predicting short-sighted next-action, which struggle with long-horizon trajectory tasks due to incremental deviations. To address this problem, we propose a plug-in framework named \method that effectively empowers off-the-shelf VLAs with the capability of foreseeing future states via test-time scaling. Specifically, \method samples and rolls out possible action trajectories where involved actions are rationales to generate future states via a world model, which enables \method to foresee and reason potential outcomes and search for the optimal actions. We further leverage Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to improve search efficiency in large action spaces, where step-wise VLA predictions seed the root. Meanwhile, we introduce a confidence sampling mechanism based on Kernel Density Estimation (KDE), to enable efficient exploration in MCTS without redundant VLA queries. We evaluate intermediate states in MCTS via an offline value estimation strategy, to score predicted futures and correct deviations with long-term feedback. We conducted extensive experiments in both simulators and the real world, demonstrating that our proposed VLA-Reasoner achieves significant improvements over the state-of-the-art VLAs. Our method highlights a potential pathway toward scalable test-time computation of robotic manipulation. The project website is available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Integrating a test-time MCTS can effectively mitigate the accumulation of short-sighted errors in VLAs by allowing the model to reason about future action consequences.

Efficient action sampling via KDE, implicitly reflecting smoothed expert behaviors, and accurate image-based value estimation are critical for practical online tree search in high-dimensional action spaces.
Blending the VLA's immediate prediction with the MCTS-reasoned action, with a moderate injection strength, achieves superior performance by balancing generalist capabilities with focused foresight.
originalProblem: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models make short-sighted next-action predictions, neglecting long-horizon sequential dependencies.
This leads to incremental deviations that accumulate over time, causing task failures in complex, multi-step robotic manipulation.
Limitations in accuracy and exploration make VLAs fragile when encountering distribution shifts in real-world environments.
results: In simulation, VLA-Reasoner improved OpenVLA-SFT's average success rate by an absolute 5% on the LIBERO benchmark and enhanced Octo-Small and SpatialVLA by an average of 9.8% and 7.8% respectively on SimplerEnv.

In real-world experiments, the framework boosted OpenVLA's average success rate by an absolute 19% (from 22% to 41%) and increased π0-FAST's by 10% (from 64% to 74%) across five challenging manipulation tasks.

Ablation studies confirmed that KDE-based action sampling (91.5% success) outperformed Gaussian noise sampling (85.0%), and image-based value estimation (91.5%) was more effective than token-based approaches (87.0%).
solution: VLA-Reasoner is introduced as a plug-in framework that empowers off-the-shelf VLAs with foresight via online Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) during test time.
It employs a learned world model to simulate future states and action trajectories, while Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) efficiently generates plausible action candidates.
A vision-based value network provides dense feedback for evaluating intermediate, model-generated states, guiding the MCTS towards optimal actions.
paper_summary.summary: VLA-Reasoner enhances Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models with test-time reasoning through online Monte Carlo Tree Search, simulating future states to select optimal actions. This framework improves VLA performance in long-horizon robotic manipulation tasks, demonstrating an absolute 19% increase in real-world success rates for OpenVLA and consistent gains in simulated environments.

title: VLA-Reasoner: Empowering Vision-Language-Action Models with Reasoning via Online Monte Carlo Tree Search

abstract:
Predicting and executing a sequence of actions without intermediate
replanning, known as action chunking, is increasingly used in robot learning
from human demonstrations. Yet, its effects on the learned policy remain
inconsistent: some studies find it crucial for achieving strong results, while
others observe decreased performance. In this paper, we first dissect how
action chunking impacts the divergence between a learner and a demonstrator. We
find that action chunking allows the learner to better capture the temporal
dependencies in demonstrations but at the cost of reduced reactivity to
unexpected states. To address this tradeoff, we propose Bidirectional Decoding
(BID), a test-time inference algorithm that bridges action chunking with
closed-loop adaptation. At each timestep, BID samples multiple candidate
predictions and searches for the optimal one based on two criteria: (i)
backward coherence, which favors samples that align with previous decisions;
(ii) forward contrast, which seeks samples of high likelihood for future plans.
By coupling decisions within and across action chunks, BID promotes both
long-term consistency and short-term reactivity. Experimental results show that
our method boosts the performance of two state-of-the-art generative policies
across seven simulation benchmarks and two real-world tasks. Code and videos
are available at this https URL

keyInsights: Action chunking in robot learning presents an inherent "consistency-reactivity trade-off," where longer action horizons provide consistency but reduce reactivity to environmental changes, and vice-versa.

Bidirectional Decoding (BID) leverages both "backward coherence" (maintaining consistency with past decisions) and "forward contrast" (selecting optimal future plans by comparing to expert/suboptimal samples) to overcome this trade-off.
Applying decoding strategies from large language models to low-level robotic control via sample comparison can significantly improve robustness and performance without requiring value functions.
originalProblem: Generative behavioral cloning policies, which predict sequences of actions (action chunks), show inconsistent performance across studies, sometimes improving and sometimes degrading results.
Traditional behavioral cloning often struggles with temporal dependencies and reactivity to unexpected environmental changes, leading to compounding errors.
There is a lack of a principled method to reconcile the need for long-term strategic consistency with short-term adaptability in robot learning from demonstrations.
results: Bidirectional Decoding consistently improved the performance of Diffusion Policy, achieving a 32% relative improvement over standard closed-loop execution across seven simulation manipulation tasks.
The method demonstrated significant robustness to stochastic noise and achieved a 2x higher success rate than baselines in real-world dynamic object manipulation tasks (e.g., grasping a moving cup).

BID is compatible with multiple state-of-the-art generative policies (Diffusion Policy, VQ-BeT) and can be combined with existing inference methods like EMA for further performance gains (46% relative improvement when combined).
solution: Introduces Bidirectional Decoding (BID), a model-agnostic test-time inference algorithm that samples multiple candidate action chunks at each timestep.

Selects the optimal action chunk by minimizing a combined objective function that considers "backward coherence" (alignment with the previously committed action chunk) and "forward contrast" (distinguishing high-likelihood expert samples from low-likelihood ones).

The forward contrast objective compares candidate chunks against "positive" samples from a strong policy and "negative" samples from a weaker policy to bias selection towards optimal, expert-like behaviors.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Stanford University developed Bidirectional Decoding (BID), a test-time inference algorithm that enhances the performance and robustness of generative behavioral cloning policies by addressing the consistency-reactivity trade-off in action chunking. BID selects optimal action sequences by combining backward coherence with previous decisions and forward contrast against positive and negative policy samples, achieving a 32% relative improvement over baselines in simulation and demonstrating effectiveness in real-world dynamic object manipulation.

title: Bidirectional Decoding: Improving Action Chunking via Guided Test-Time  Sampling

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have recently demonstrated strong performance across embodied tasks. Modern VLAs commonly employ diffusion action experts to efficiently generate high-precision continuous action chunks, while auto-regressive generation can be slower and less accurate at low-level control. Yet auto-regressive paradigms still provide complementary priors that can improve robustness and generalization in out-of-distribution environments. To leverage both paradigms, we propose Action-Draft-and-Verify (ADV): diffusion action expert drafts multiple candidate action chunks, and the VLM selects one by scoring all candidates in a single forward pass with a perplexity-style metric. Under matched backbones, training data, and action-chunk length, ADV improves success rate by +4.3 points in simulation and +19.7 points in real-world over diffusion-based baseline, with a single-pass VLM reranking overhead.

keyInsights: VLM verification primarily acts as a 'failure-mode filter,' effectively discarding suboptimal or erroneous action drafts proposed by the diffusion expert, rather than consistently identifying the single optimal trajectory.
The VLM's perplexity-style score is a reliable metric for filtering candidate actions, showing an inverse correlation with task success and demonstrating robustness to noisy inputs.
Representing action chunks as text-aligned tokens via Textual FAST enhances the stability and reliability of the VLM's perplexity-based verification by aligning with its pre-training distribution.
originalProblem: Diffusion-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, while precise, exhibit reduced robustness and underfitting behaviors (e.g., fewer recovery attempts, more collisions) in out-of-distribution (OOD) environments.
Auto-regressive VLA models demonstrate more stable corrective behavior under distribution shifts but generally achieve lower success rates and can be slower than diffusion-based methods.
Existing hybrid VLA approaches often involve complex co-training or direct prediction fusion, which can be sensitive to disagreements between different model predictions.
results: ADV consistently improved success rates, averaging +4.3 percentage points in simulation (e.g., +3.5 on RoboTwin2.0 Hard OOD tasks) and +17.1 points in real-world settings compared to diffusion-based inference.

In OOD environments (RoboTwin2.0 Hard), ADV significantly reduced average success steps by 99.9, increased recovery attempts by 1.6, and reduced pre-grasp environmental collisions by 26.5 percentage points.
Textual FAST achieved a 96.8% success rate on the LIBERO benchmark, outperforming other tokenization methods like FAST (94.4%) and VLA-0 (94.9%) for VLM verification.
solution: The Action-Draft-and-Verify (ADV) framework co-trains an auto-regressive VLM backbone and a lightweight diffusion action expert using a combined loss function.

A two-stage inference procedure: a 'Draft' phase where the diffusion expert generates multiple candidate action chunks, and a 'Verify' phase where the VLM scores these candidates based on a perplexity-style metric and selects the best one.
Introduced Textual FAST, a novel discrete action tokenization method that compresses continuous action chunks into text-aligned tokens, improving the VLM's reliability for perplexity-based scoring.
paper_summary.summary: The Action-Draft-and-Verify (ADV) framework enhances Vision-Language-Action models by using a diffusion expert to draft multiple action candidates which an auto-regressive VLM then verifies and selects based on perplexity scores. This approach improved success rates by an average of +4.3% in simulation and +19.7% in real-world tasks, particularly in out-of-distribution environments, by filtering suboptimal actions.

title: Action Draft and Verify: A Self-Verifying Framework for Vision-Language-Action Model

abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have significantly advanced robotic manipulation by integrating vision-language models (VLMs), and action decoders into a unified architecture. However, their deployment on resource-constrained edge devices, such as mobile robots or embedded systems (e.g., Jetson Orin Nano), remains challenging due to high computational demands, especially in real-world scenarios where power, latency, and computational resources are critical. To close this gap, we introduce Nano-scale Vision-Language Action (NanoVLA), a family of lightweight VLA architectures that achieve high performance with minimal resources. Our core innovations include: (1) vision-language decoupling that moves conventional early vision and language inputs fusion in VLM to late stage, achieving better performance while enabling caching and reduce inference overhead and latency; (2) long-short action chunking to ensure smooth, coherent multi-step planning without sacrificing real-time responsiveness; (3) dynamic routing that adaptively assigns lightweight or heavy backbones based on task complexity, further optimizing inference efficiency. Experimental results on several benchmarks, as well as real-world deployments, demonstrate that NanoVLA achieves up to 52x faster inference on edge devices compared to previous state-of-the-art VLA models, with 98% less parameters while maintaining or surpassing their task accuracy and generalization. Ablation studies confirm that our decoupling strategy preserves cross-task transferability, and the routing module enhances cost-performance trade-offs, enabling practical, high-precision robotic manipulation on resource-constrained hardware.

keyInsights: Efficient VLA inference on edge devices can be achieved by fundamentally re-architecting the process to be more modular, context-aware, and adaptive, rather than simply shrinking large models.
Decoupling vision and language processing allows for significant inference speedups through caching, as language instructions often remain static across multiple control steps.

Adaptive resource allocation, through dynamic routing, ensures that computational capacity is matched to task difficulty, preventing over-computation for simple tasks and providing sufficient capacity for complex ones.
originalProblem: High computational demands of state-of-the-art VLA models prevent their practical deployment on resource-constrained edge devices due to slow inference and high power consumption.

Existing action generation strategies often lead to "jerky or brittle motions" and unstable behaviors for complex, long-horizon tasks due to trade-offs between temporal coherence and real-time responsiveness.
A single, fixed-capacity VLA backbone struggles to optimally handle the wide spectrum of robotic tasks, leading to inefficient resource allocation.
results: Achieved up to 52x faster inference (higher FPS) on a Jetson Orin Nano compared to OpenVLA, while also improving the success rate by +13.8%.
Outperformed larger VLA models (e.g., OpenVLA, π0) on LIBERO simulated benchmarks, achieving an average success rate of 84.1% with significantly fewer total parameters (296M vs. 7.5B).

Demonstrated strong generalization to out-of-domain tasks and robustness to environmental changes (e.g., varying lighting) in real-world LeRobot tasks, showing SR improvements between 8.5% and 42.1% over baselines.
solution: Implements Vision-Language Decoupling with Caching, processing vision and language inputs independently and caching instruction embeddings to eliminate redundant computation.
Introduces Long-Short Action Chunking (LSAC), which trains policies on long action sequences for temporal coherence but executes only short windows during inference for real-time adaptability.
Incorporates Dynamic Routing with a lightweight VLA router that uses Bayesian success modeling to adaptively select between different language backbones based on task complexity, optimizing compute.
paper_summary.summary: NANOVLA introduces a framework for generalist robotic policies, enabling advanced vision-language-action (VLA) models to run on resource-constrained edge devices by decoupling vision and language processing, employing long-short action chunking, and dynamically routing computational resources. The framework achieves up to 52x faster inference on a Jetson Orin Nano and improves success rates on simulated and real-world tasks compared to larger VLA baselines.

title: NanoVLA: Routing Decoupled Vision-Language Understanding for Nano-sized Generalist Robotic Policies

abstract: To improve efficiency and temporal coherence, Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often predict action chunks; however, this action chunking harms reactivity under inference delay and long horizons. We introduce Asynchronous Action Chunk Correction (A2C2), which is a lightweight real-time chunk correction head that runs every control step and adds a time-aware correction to any off-the-shelf VLA's action chunk. The module combines the latest observation, the predicted action from VLA (base action), a positional feature that encodes the index of the base action within the chunk, and some features from the base policy, then outputs a per-step correction. This preserves the base model's competence while restoring closed-loop responsiveness. The approach requires no retraining of the base policy and is orthogonal to asynchronous execution schemes such as Real Time Chunking (RTC). On the dynamic Kinetix task suite (12 tasks) and LIBERO Spatial, our method yields consistent success rate improvements across increasing delays and execution horizons (+23% point and +7% point respectively, compared to RTC), and also improves robustness for long horizons even with zero injected delay. Since the correction head is small and fast, there is minimal overhead compared to the inference of large VLA models. These results indicate that A2C2 is an effective, plug-in mechanism for deploying high-capacity chunking policies in real-time control.

keyInsights: Real-time adaptation within VLA action chunks can be achieved effectively by a lightweight residual correction module, without the need to retrain the computationally intensive base VLA model.
Explicitly incorporating temporal awareness through positional features within the correction mechanism is crucial for refining actions accurately across the entire chunk horizon.
The computational overhead of the A2C2 correction head is minimal (~20 times faster than base VLA inference), enabling its deployment in high-frequency control loops for real-time robotic systems.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models incur significant inference latency when generating action chunks, creating delays between observations and robotic actions.
Action chunking, while improving throughput, leads to open-loop execution within chunks, preventing real-time adaptation to new sensory information during execution.

The combination of latency and open-loop chunk execution causes robots to accumulate errors, miss critical environmental cues, and fail tasks requiring rapid, reactive adjustments in dynamic environments.
results: A2C2 consistently increased success rates in dynamic Kinetix tasks, achieving up to a +35% improvement over naive baselines and a +23% point increase compared to Real Time Chunking (RTC) under increasing delays.
In the LIBERO Spatial benchmark, A2C2 improved success rates by +17% (84% vs 67%) under challenging conditions of long horizons and delays, and by +9.4% even with zero delay but long horizons.

The A2C2 correction head demonstrated an average inference time of 4.7 ms per step, roughly 20 times faster than the base SmolVLA policy (101 ms), validating its practical viability for real-time deployment.
solution: A2C2 introduces a lightweight correction head that refines VLA-predicted actions at every control step by incorporating the latest sensory observations.

The correction head predicts a residual action at each control step, based on the current observation, the corresponding base action from the VLA chunk, and a positional feature encoding its relative index.
The correction head is trained using supervised learning on the difference between expert actions and the base VLA's outputs, without requiring retraining of the large, pre-trained VLA policy.
paper_summary.summary: The University of Tokyo researchers introduced Asynchronous Action Chunk Correction (A2C2), a lightweight, real-time method that enhances the responsiveness of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models in robotic control. A2C2 significantly improved task success rates in dynamic manipulation and locomotion tasks by up to 35% compared to baselines, even under high inference delays and long action chunk horizons.

title: Leave No Observation Behind: Real-time Correction for VLA Action Chunks

abstract: Robotic imitation learning faces a fundamental trade-off between modeling long-horizon dependencies and enabling fine-grained closed-loop control. Existing fixed-frequency action chunking approaches struggle to achieve both. Building on this insight, we propose HiPolicy, a hierarchical multi-frequency action chunking framework that jointly predicts action sequences at different frequencies to capture both coarse high-level plans and precise reactive motions. We extract and fuse hierarchical features from history observations aligned to each frequency for multi-frequency chunk generation, and introduce an entropy-guided execution mechanism that adaptively balances long-horizon planning with fine-grained control based on action uncertainty. Experiments on diverse simulated benchmarks and real-world manipulation tasks show that HiPolicy can be seamlessly integrated into existing 2D and 3D generative policies, delivering consistent improvements in performance while significantly enhancing execution efficiency.

keyInsights: Mimicking human motor control by explicitly modeling actions at multiple frequencies effectively reconciles the trade-off between long-horizon planning and fine-grained reactive control in robotic policies.

Fusing features across different temporal resolutions (both frequency-specific and global) is crucial for a comprehensive policy that can interpret high-level goals and execute precise, short-term movements.

An adaptive execution mechanism, driven by action entropy, can significantly improve both the efficiency and robustness of robotic policies by dynamically adjusting the control granularity based on the perceived uncertainty of the task state.
originalProblem: Existing action chunking methods in robotic imitation learning face a fundamental trade-off: low-frequency chunks capture long-horizon dependencies but lack temporal resolution for precise control, while high-frequency chunks allow fine-grained adjustments but struggle with long-horizon planning.
Conventional imitation learning approaches grapple with error accumulation and difficulty in modeling long-horizon, non-Markovian behaviors.

Prior hierarchical approaches in robot learning have primarily focused on multi-level perceptual input or specific slow-fast policies, with few investigating a hierarchical structure for both perception and action at different temporal resolutions.
results: HiPolicy achieved relative success rate advantages of 62% and 44% over Diffusion Policy (DP) and DP3 on RoboTwin 1.0 and 2.0 benchmarks, respectively, showing strong performance in high-precision and super-long-horizon tasks.

Real-world experiments demonstrated a 42% higher average success rate and 14% faster average execution speed compared to the DP baseline across 8 manipulation tasks, successfully completing non-Markovian tasks like 'Close Microwave Door' where DP failed.

Ablation studies confirmed that hierarchical observation conditioning, multi-frequency feature fusion, and the core hierarchical frequency structure are critical components, with entropy-guided execution improving speed by 25% with minimal impact on success rates.
solution: HiPolicy jointly encodes multi-frequency observation histories and predicts a single hierarchical multi-frequency action chunk using a diffusion-based model.

It employs Hierarchical FiLM Fusion for frequency-specific conditioning of action features and a Global Feature Fusion module, using cross-attention, to enable information exchange across different frequencies.

An entropy-guided adaptive execution strategy dynamically selects the appropriate action frequency during inference based on the uncertainty of the predicted action chunk, balancing precision requirements with execution speed.
paper_summary.summary: HiPolicy introduces a hierarchical multi-frequency action chunking framework for robotic imitation learning, enabling policies to simultaneously capture long-horizon dependencies and execute fine-grained control. The framework, incorporating an entropy-guided adaptive execution, significantly improves task success rates and execution speed across diverse simulated and real-world manipulation tasks.

title: HiPolicy: Hierarchical Multi-Frequency Action Chunking for Policy Learning

abstract: Action chunking is a widely adopted approach in Learning from Demonstration (LfD). By modeling multi-step action chunks rather than single-step actions, action chunking significantly enhances modeling capabilities for human expert policies. However, the reduced decision frequency restricts the utilization of recent observations, degrading reactivity - particularly evident in the inadequate adaptation to sensor noise and dynamic environmental changes. Existing efforts to address this issue have primarily resorted to trading off reactivity against decision consistency, without achieving both. To address this limitation, we propose a novel algorithm, Temporal Action Selector (TAS), which caches predicted action chunks from multiple timesteps and dynamically selects the optimal action through a lightweight selector network. TAS achieves balanced optimization across three critical dimensions: reactivity, decision consistency, and motion coherence. Experiments across multiple tasks with diverse base policies show that TAS significantly improves success rates - yielding an absolute gain of up to 73.3%. Furthermore, integrating TAS as a base policy with residual reinforcement learning (RL) substantially enhances training efficiency and elevates the performance plateau. Experiments in both simulation and physical robots confirm the method's efficacy.
title: Temporal Action Selection for Action Chunking

abstract: Action chunking enables Vision Language Action (VLA) models to run in real time, but naive chunked execution often exhibits discontinuities at chunk boundaries. Real-Time Chunking (RTC) alleviates this issue but is external to the policy, leading to spurious multimodal switching and trajectories that are not intrinsically smooth. We propose Legato, a training-time continuation method for action-chunked flow-based VLA policies. Specifically, Legato initializes denoising from a schedule-shaped mixture of known actions and noise, exposing the model to partial action information. Moreover, Legato reshapes the learned flow dynamics to ensure that the denoising process remains consistent between training and inference under per-step guidance. Legato further uses randomized schedule condition during training to support varying inference delays and achieve controllable smoothness. Empirically, Legato produces smoother trajectories and reduces spurious multimodal switching during execution, leading to less hesitation and shorter task completion time. Extensive real-world experiments show that Legato consistently outperforms RTC across five manipulation tasks, achieving approximately 10% improvements in both trajectory smoothness and task completion time.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Per-step guidance applied throughout the denoising process is necessary to maintain consistency in action chunk overlap regions, as single-shot guidance results in significant drift.
Robust action chunk continuation is best achieved by natively integrating it into the policy's learned flow dynamics during training, rather than using external, inference-time corrections.
Deriving and ensuring training-inference consistency between the learned velocity field and the dynamics of iterative, per-step guidance is critical for effective policy behavior.
Randomized schedule conditioning enables a single policy to generalize efficiently across diverse real-world inference latencies and varying requirements for continuation strength.
originalProblem: Naive action chunking in VLA models causes discontinuities and "spurious multimodal switching" at chunk boundaries, leading to jerky robot movements and increased task completion times.

Current inference-time continuation methods, such as Real-Time Chunking (RTC), fail to fully integrate smooth transitions into the policy's learned dynamics, resulting in persistent hesitation and suboptimal efficiency.
Policies lack a native, learned mechanism for robust chunk-to-chunk continuation, leading to inconsistent behavior and reduced reliability in real-world robotic manipulation.
results: Legato significantly reduced task completion times (e.g., ~20% faster on Pour) and improved task success rates compared to Real-Time Chunking (RTC) across five robotic manipulation tasks.

It achieved superior trajectory smoothness, evidenced by consistently lower NSPARC values and substantially reduced chunk-overlap RMSE (e.g., 7.64 to 5.14 on Pour), indicating better chunk-to-chunk stitching.
Legato outperformed Training-time RTC on the "pour" task, demonstrating higher task scores, shorter completion times, and improved smoothness metrics.

The method showed robustness to variations in execution stride, ramp length, and inference delay, and generalized effectively across different VLA model backbones ("
0" and "
0.5").
solution: A training-time mechanism constructs an "action-noise mixture" based on a horizon-wise guidance schedule, implicitly integrating continuation into the flow-matching path's initialization.
The exact continuous-time dynamics corresponding to iterative, per-step guidance are derived to ensure consistency between the training objective and the inference process.

The policy's learned velocity field is reshaped during training to predict a target velocity that aligns with these per-step guided dynamics, thereby embedding continuation natively within the policy.
Schedule randomization and explicit conditioning on the guidance schedule are applied during training, enabling the model to adapt to variable inference delays and desired ramp lengths.
paper_summary.summary: Legato integrates action chunk continuation directly into flow-based Vision Language Action (VLA) policies by reshaping the learned velocity field to ensure training-inference consistency under per-step guidance. This approach yields robotic movements with significantly reduced task completion times, decreased hesitation, and enhanced trajectory smoothness across various manipulation tasks.

title: Learning Native Continuation for Action Chunking Flow Policies

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, as large foundation models for embodied control, have shown strong performance in manipulation tasks. However, their performance comes at high inference cost. To improve efficiency, recent methods adopt action chunking, which predicts a sequence of future actions for open-loop execution. Although effective for reducing computation, open-loop execution is sensitive to environmental changes and prone to error accumulation due to the lack of close-loop feedback. To address this limitation, we propose Speculative Verification for VLA Control (SV-VLA), a framework that combines efficient open-loop long-horizon planning with lightweight closed-loop online verification. Specifically, SV-VLA uses a heavy VLA as a low-frequency macro-planner to generate an action chunk together with a planning context, while a lightweight verifier continuously monitors execution based on the latest observations. Conditioned on both the current observation and the planning context, the verifier compares the planned action against a closed-loop reference action and triggers replanning only when necessary. Experiments demonstrate that SV-VLA combines the efficiency of chunked prediction with the robustness of closed-loop control, enabling efficient and reliable VLA-based control in dynamic environments. Code is available: this https URL.

keyInsights: Optimizing embodied control involves a combination of infrequent, computationally expensive planning and continuous, inexpensive verification.

The dynamic nature of embodied environments necessitates closed-loop verification, where future action validity is assessed against real-time observations, unlike static context-based verification in NLP.
A compact planning context feature extracted from the heavy VLA, combined with real-time visual observations, enables a lightweight verifier to effectively assess the consistency of an ongoing plan.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models incur high inference latency, making them difficult to deploy as high-frequency closed-loop robot controllers.
Open-loop action chunking improves efficiency by predicting action sequences but suffers from error accumulation and lack of adaptation to environmental changes due to stale observations.

Standard speculative decoding from Natural Language Processing is not directly applicable to embodied control because future action validity depends on dynamic environmental interactions, not just static context.
results: SV-VLA achieved an average success rate of 90.9% across LIBERO benchmark tasks, an 11.4% improvement over a baseline open-loop chunking method (79.5%) with a comparable chunk size of 64.
The framework demonstrated a 2.17x inference speed-up compared to a closed-loop baseline (OpenVLA-OFT K=8), effectively balancing control robustness with computational efficiency.

Ablation studies confirmed that both the planning context feature and real-time current observations are crucial for the verifier's effectiveness, and the replanning mechanism is essential for recovering from execution errors.
solution: Introduced Speculative Verification for VLA Control (SV-VLA), a framework that decouples low-frequency macro-planning from high-frequency lightweight verification.
A heavy VLA model acts as a Macro-Planner, generating long action chunks and a concise planning context feature at planning boundaries (e.g., task start or replanning events).

A lightweight Verifier continuously monitors execution, comparing planned macro actions with real-time reference actions based on current observations and the planning context, triggering replanning only when deviations exceed a threshold.
paper_summary.summary: SV-VLA, a framework from Southeast University and Nanyang Technological University, combines infrequent heavy VLA planning with continuous lightweight verification to improve robot control efficiency and robustness. On the LIBERO benchmark, it achieved a 90.9% success rate with a 2.17x speed-up, recovering 11.4% success over open-loop chunking methods.

title: Open-Loop Planning, Closed-Loop Verification: Speculative Verification for VLA

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a generalist robotic agent. However, existing VLAs are hindered by excessive parameter scales, prohibitive pre-training requirements, and limited applicability to diverse embodiments. To improve the practicality of VLAs, we propose a comprehensive benchmark and an improved baseline. First, we propose CEBench, a new benchmark spanning diverse embodiments in both simulation and the real world with consideration of domain randomization. We collect 14.4k simulated trajectories and 1.6k real-world expert-curated trajectories to support training on CEBench. Second, using CEBench as our testbed, we study three critical aspects of VLAs' practicality and offer several key findings. Informed by these findings, we introduce LLaVA-VLA, a lightweight yet powerful VLA designed for practical deployment on consumer-grade GPUs. Architecturally, it integrates a compact VLM backbone with multi-view perception, proprioceptive tokenization, and action chunking. To eliminate reliance on costly pre-training, LLaVA-VLA adopts a two-stage training paradigm including post-training and fine-tuning. Furthermore, LLaVA-VLA extends the action space to unify navigation and manipulation. Experiments across embodiments demonstrate the capabilities of generalization and versatility of LLaVA-VLA , while real-world mobile manipulation experiments establish it as the first end-to-end VLA model for mobile manipulation. We will open-source all datasets, codes, and checkpoints upon acceptance to foster reproducibility and future research.
title: Rethinking the Practicality of Vision-language-action Model: A Comprehensive Benchmark and An Improved Baseline

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated strong multi-modal reasoning capabilities, enabling direct action generation from visual perception and language instructions in an end-to-end manner. However, their substantial computational cost poses a challenge for real-time robotic control, where rapid decision-making is essential. This paper introduces VLA-Cache, a training-free inference acceleration method that reduces computational overhead by adaptively caching and reusing static visual tokens across frames. Exploiting the temporal continuity in robotic manipulation, VLA-Cache identifies minimally changed tokens between adjacent frames and reuses their cached key-value representations, thereby circumventing redundant computations. Additionally, to maintain action precision, VLA-Cache selectively re-computes task-relevant tokens that are environmentally sensitive, ensuring the fidelity of critical visual information. To further optimize efficiency, we introduce a layer adaptive token reusing strategy that dynamically adjusts the reuse ratio based on attention concentration across decoder layers, prioritizing critical tokens for recomputation. Extensive experiments on two simulation platforms (LIBERO and SIMPLER) and a real-world robotic system demonstrate that VLA-Cache achieves up to 1.7x speedup in CUDA latency and a 15% increase in control frequency, with negligible loss on task success rate. The code and videos can be found at our project page: this https URL.
title: VLA-Cache: Efficient Vision-Language-Action Manipulation via Adaptive Token Caching

abstract: Recent vision-language-action models (VLAs) build upon pretrained vision-language models and leverage diverse robot datasets to demonstrate strong task execution, language following ability, and semantic generalization. Despite these successes, VLAs struggle with novel robot setups and require fine-tuning to achieve good performance, yet how to most effectively fine-tune them is unclear given many possible strategies. In this work, we study key VLA adaptation design choices such as different action decoding schemes, action representations, and learning objectives for fine-tuning, using OpenVLA as our representative base model. Our empirical analysis informs an Optimized Fine-Tuning (OFT) recipe that integrates parallel decoding, action chunking, a continuous action representation, and a simple L1 regression-based learning objective to altogether improve inference efficiency, policy performance, and flexibility in the model's input-output specifications. We propose OpenVLA-OFT, an instantiation of this recipe, which sets a new state of the art on the LIBERO simulation benchmark, significantly boosting OpenVLA's average success rate across four task suites from 76.5% to 97.1% while increasing action generation throughput by 26$\times$. In real-world evaluations, our fine-tuning recipe enables OpenVLA to successfully execute dexterous, high-frequency control tasks on a bimanual ALOHA robot and outperform other VLAs ($\pi_0$ and RDT-1B) fine-tuned using their default recipes, as well as strong imitation learning policies trained from scratch (Diffusion Policy and ACT) by up to 15% (absolute) in average success rate. We release code for OFT and pretrained model checkpoints at this https URL.
title: Fine-Tuning Vision-Language-Action Models: Optimizing Speed and Success

title: Fine-Tuning Vision-Language-Action Models: Optimizing Speed and Success

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models demonstrate remarkable potential for generalizable robotic manipulation. The performance of VLA models can be improved by integrating with action chunking, a critical technique for effective control. However, action chunking linearly scales up action dimensions in VLA models with increased chunking sizes. This reduces the inference efficiency. To tackle this problem, we propose PD-VLA, the first parallel decoding framework for VLA models integrated with action chunking. Our framework reformulates autoregressive decoding as a nonlinear system solved by parallel fixed-point iterations. This approach preserves model performance with mathematical guarantees while significantly improving decoding speed. In addition, it enables training-free acceleration without architectural changes, as well as seamless synergy with existing acceleration techniques. Extensive simulations validate that our PD-VLA maintains competitive success rates while achieving 2.52 times execution frequency on manipulators (with 7 degrees of freedom) compared with the fundamental VLA model. Furthermore, we experimentally identify the most effective settings for acceleration. Finally, real-world experiments validate its high applicability across different tasks.

organization_info.name: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

title: Accelerating Vision-Language-Action Model Integrated with Action Chunking via Parallel Decoding

abstract: Recent advances in Visual-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown promising potential for robotic manipulation tasks. However, real-world robotic tasks often involve long-horizon, multi-step problem-solving and require generalization for continual skill acquisition, extending beyond single actions or skills. These challenges present significant barriers for existing VLA models, which use monolithic action decoders trained on aggregated data, resulting in poor scalability. To address these challenges, we propose AtomicVLA, a unified planning-and-execution framework that jointly generates task-level plans, atomic skill abstractions, and fine-grained actions. AtomicVLA constructs a scalable atomic skill library through a Skill-Guided Mixture-of-Experts (SG-MoE), where each expert specializes in mastering generic yet precise atomic skills. Furthermore, we introduce a flexible routing encoder that automatically assigns dedicated atomic experts to new skills, enabling continual learning. We validate our approach through extensive experiments. In simulation, AtomicVLA outperforms $\pi_{0}$ by 2.4\% on LIBERO, 10\% on LIBERO-LONG, and outperforms $\pi_{0}$ and $\pi_{0.5}$ by 0.22 and 0.25 in average task length on CALVIN. Additionally, our AtomicVLA consistently surpasses baselines by 18.3\% and 21\% in real-world long-horizon tasks and continual learning. These results highlight the effectiveness of atomic skill abstraction and dynamic expert composition for long-horizon and lifelong robotic tasks. The project page is \href{this https URL}{here}.

keyInsights: Integrating task planning and action generation into a unified model, which adaptively switches between modalities, enhances coherent decision-making and performance for complex, multi-step tasks.

Specializing action decoders into a mixture of experts, guided by explicit semantic atomic skill abstractions, improves execution fidelity and effectively mitigates interference between different skills.

A modular and expandable skill expert library, coupled with a specific router initialization strategy, allows for efficient continual learning while preserving the performance of established skills, addressing catastrophic forgetting.
Accurate, physics-informed decomposition of robot trajectories into atomic actions is crucial for developing robust task planning and execution policies.
originalProblem: Existing Visual-Language-Action (VLA) models struggle with complex, long-horizon, multi-step robotic tasks, often lacking robust sequential reasoning capabilities.
Current VLA models face limitations in scalable continual skill acquisition, with monolithic action decoders leading to catastrophic forgetting during the learning of new skills.
Skill interference is a common issue when training VLA models on diverse tasks, degrading overall performance.
results: AtomicVLA achieved a 95.2% success rate on the LIBERO-LONG benchmark, demonstrating a 10% improvement over the \u03c00 baseline, and outperformed baselines by up to 20% on real-world long-horizon tasks.
It successfully mitigated catastrophic forgetting during continual learning, showing only a 1.3% average performance decrease on base tasks, compared to a 15% decrease experienced by baseline models.
The framework exhibited efficient new skill acquisition, with AtomicVLA* achieving 70% success for a newly introduced 'open' skill, surpassing \u03c00.5's 55% under the same training steps.
Ablation studies confirmed the effectiveness of the Skill-Guided MoE, achieving 95.2% success on LIBERO-LONG, outperforming token-level MoE (6.6% better) and MoDE (5.7% better) variants.
solution: AtomicVLA unifies 'thinking' (high-level task planning and atomic skill abstraction) and 'acting' (low-level action execution) within an adaptive end-to-end framework.
It introduces a Skill-Guided Mixture-of-Experts (SG-MoE) architecture that dynamically routes multimodal inputs to specialized atomic skill experts, extending a VLA foundation model.
The framework facilitates continual learning by modularly adding new skill experts and routing branches, specifically designed to prevent catastrophic forgetting of previously acquired abilities.
A physics-informed data generation pipeline, using principal-axis analysis and VLM refinement, creates accurate and semantically meaningful atomic action annotations for training.
paper_summary.summary: AtomicVLA is a robotic framework that unifies high-level task planning and low-level action execution, enabling efficient long-horizon task completion and scalable continual learning. The system demonstrates improved success rates and effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting in both simulated and real-world manipulation tasks.

title: AtomicVLA: Unlocking the Potential of Atomic Skill Learning in Robots

The performance of VLA models can be improved by integrating with action chunking, a critical technique for effective control. However, action chunking linearly scales up action dimensions in VLA models with increased chunking sizes. This reduces the inference efficiency. To tackle this problem, we propose PD-VLA, the first parallel decoding framework for VLA models integrated with action chunking. Our framework reformulates autoregressive decoding as a nonlinear system solved by parallel fixed-point iterations. This approach preserves model performance with mathematical guarantees while significantly improving decoding speed. In addition, it enables training-free acceleration without architectural changes, as well as seamless synergy with existing acceleration techniques. Extensive simulations validate that our PD-VLA maintains competitive success rates while achieving 2.52 times execution frequency on manipulators (with 7 degrees of freedom) compared with the fundamental VLA model. Furthermore, we experimentally identify the most effective settings for acceleration. Finally, real-world experiments validate its high applicability across different tasks.
title: PD-VLA: Accelerating Vision-Language-Action Model Integrated with Action Chunking via Parallel Decoding

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a generalist robotic agent. title: Rethinking the Practicality of Vision-language-action Model: A Comprehensive Benchmark and An Improved Baseline

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models adapt large vision-language backbones to map images and instructions into robot actions. However, prevailing VLAs either generate actions auto-regressively in a fixed left-to-right order or attach separate MLP or diffusion heads outside the backbone, leading to fragmented information pathways and specialized training requirements that hinder a unified, scalable architecture. We present Discrete Diffusion VLA, a unified-transformer policy that models discretized action chunks with discrete diffusion. The design retains diffusion's progressive refinement paradigm while remaining natively compatible with the discrete token interface of VLMs. Our method achieves an adaptive decoding order that resolves easy action elements before harder ones and uses secondary re-masking to revisit uncertain predictions across refinement rounds, which improves consistency and enables robust error correction. This unified decoder preserves pre-trained vision-language priors, supports parallel decoding, breaks the autoregressive bottleneck, and reduces the number of function evaluations. Discrete Diffusion VLA achieves 96.3% avg. success rates on LIBERO, 71.2% visual matching on SimplerEnv-Fractal and 54.2% overall on SimplerEnv-Bridge, improving over autoregressive, MLP decoder and continuous diffusion baselines. These findings indicate that discrete-diffusion VLA supports precise action modeling and consistent training, laying groundwork for scaling VLA to larger models and datasets. keyInsights: Model performance is not strictly proportional to parameter scale; lightweight designs like LLaVA-VLA can achieve comparable or superior results to larger, pre-trained models through intelligent architectural choices.

Extensive cross-embodiment large-scale pre-training is not essential; effective vision-to-action mapping can be established through post-training on in-domain, multi-task data, followed by task-specific fine-tuning.
A unified action space using discrete direction and value tokens allows for stable and flexible switching between navigation and manipulation modalities, enabling complex mobile manipulation tasks.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models suffer from excessive parameter scales, making them computationally intensive and difficult to deploy on resource-constrained platforms.
Current VLA models require prohibitive pre-training on vast robotic datasets, leading to high computational costs and limiting accessibility.
Most VLAs demonstrate limited applicability, primarily in fixed-base manipulation settings, lacking versatility for cross-embodiment tasks such as mobile manipulation.
results: LLaVA-VLA (0.5B parameters) achieved competitive or superior performance on single-arm (CALVIN) and bimanual (RoboTwin) manipulation tasks compared to much larger (3B, 7B) and pre-trained models.
The model demonstrated robust performance in real-world fixed-base bimanual manipulation, maintaining higher success rates under domain randomization than baselines (ACT, TinyVLA).
LLaVA-VLA became the first end-to-end VLA model to successfully handle real-world mobile manipulation tasks, outperforming baselines in tasks requiring both navigation and precise manipulation.
solution: A comprehensive benchmark, CEBench, was proposed to evaluate VLA models across diverse robotic embodiments (single-arm, bimanual, mobile bimanual) in both simulated and real-world scenarios, incorporating explicit domain randomization.
LLaVA-VLA, a lightweight (0.5B parameters) and pre-training-free VLA model, was developed using a compact LLaVA-OneVision backbone and a two-stage training approach.
LLaVA-VLA integrates multi-view perception, proprioceptive tokenization, action chunking, and a unified hybrid action space designed for seamless navigation and manipulation.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou) developed CEBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models across diverse robotic embodiments and domain randomization. They also introduced LLaVA-VLA, a lightweight, pre-training-free baseline that achieves competitive performance on various manipulation tasks, including being the first end-to-end VLA model capable of mobile manipulation.

title: Rethinking the Practicality of Vision-language-action Model: A Comprehensive Benchmark and An Improved Baseline

abstract: Recent large-scale Vision Language Action (VLA) models have shown superior performance in robotic manipulation tasks guided by natural language. However, current VLA models suffer from two drawbacks: (i) generation of massive tokens leading to high inference latency and increased training cost, and (ii) insufficient utilization of generated actions resulting in potential performance loss. To address these issues, we develop a training framework to finetune VLA models for generating significantly fewer action tokens with high parallelism, effectively reducing inference latency and training cost. Furthermore, we introduce an inference optimization technique with a novel voting-based ensemble strategy to combine current and previous action predictions, improving the utilization of generated actions and overall performance. Our results demonstrate that we achieve superior performance compared with state-of-the-art VLA models, achieving significantly higher success rates and 39$\times$ faster inference than OpenVLA with 46 Hz throughput on edge platforms, demonstrating practical deployability. The code is available at this https URL.
title: VOTE: Vision-Language-Action Optimization with Trajectory Ensemble Voting

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have become a cornerstone in robotic policy learning, leveraging large-scale multimodal data for robust and scalable control. However, existing VLA frameworks primarily address short-horizon tasks, and their effectiveness on long-horizon, multi-step robotic manipulation remains limited due to challenges in skill chaining and subtask dependencies. In this work, we introduce Long-VLA, the first end-to-end VLA model specifically designed for long-horizon robotic tasks. Our approach features a novel phase-aware input masking strategy that adaptively segments each subtask into moving and interaction phases, enabling the model to focus on phase-relevant sensory cues and enhancing subtask compatibility. This unified strategy preserves the scalability and data efficiency of VLA training, and our architecture-agnostic module can be seamlessly integrated into existing VLA models. We further propose the L-CALVIN benchmark to systematically evaluate long-horizon manipulation. Extensive experiments on both simulated and real-world tasks demonstrate that Long-VLA significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods, establishing a new baseline for long-horizon robotic control.
title: Long-VLA: Unleashing Long-Horizon Capability of Vision Language Action Model for Robot Manipulation

In this work, we introduce Long-VLA, the first end-to-end VLA model specifically designed for long-horizon robotic tasks. Our approach features a novel phase-aware input masking strategy that adaptively segments each subtask into moving and interaction phases, enabling the model to focus on phase-relevant sensory cues and enhancing subtask compatibility. This unified strategy preserves the scalability and data efficiency of VLA training, and our architecture-agnostic module can be seamlessly integrated into existing VLA models. We further propose the L-CALVIN benchmark to systematically evaluate long-horizon manipulation. Extensive experiments on both simulated and real-world tasks demonstrate that Long-VLA significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods, establishing a new baseline for long-horizon robotic control.

title: Long-VLA: Unleashing Long-Horizon Capability of Vision Language Action Model for Robot Manipulation

abstract: Recent large-scale Vision Language Action (VLA) models have shown superior performance in robotic manipulation tasks guided by natural language. However, current VLA models suffer from two drawbacks: (i) generation of massive tokens leading to high inference latency and increased training cost, and (ii) insufficient utilization of generated actions resulting in potential performance loss. To address these issues, we develop a training framework to finetune VLA models for generating significantly fewer action tokens with high parallelism, effectively reducing inference latency and training cost. Furthermore, we introduce an inference optimization technique with a novel voting-based ensemble strategy to combine current and previous action predictions, improving the utilization of generated actions and overall performance. Our results demonstrate that we achieve superior performance compared with state-of-the-art VLA models, achieving significantly higher success rates and 39$\times$ faster inference than OpenVLA with 46 Hz throughput on edge platforms, demonstrating practical deployability.
